paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 1: Daily Life & Communication
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Grammar mini-lessons

This unit has several small grammar lessons. For each one, read what it means, look at how to form it, study the mistake to avoid, remember the tip, and then try the practice. Check your answers in the Answer Key at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1:  Verb Patterns
What it means and why it matters

A verb pattern is the way we join a verb to the word that comes after it. Some verbs are followed by to + another verb, and some are followed by a verb ending in -ing. Getting the pattern right makes your sentences clear.
How to form it
Some verbs take to + verb: agree, want, decide, plan, need. → “She agrees to send the mail.”
Some verbs take verb + -ing: enjoy, love, like, finish. → “They enjoy playing football.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “She agrees help her friend.”  ✗
Fix: The verb “agree” needs to: “She agrees to help her friend.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of the main verb as a key that opens the door to its partner. Learn which verbs carry the to key and which carry the -ing key.
Practice 1
Choose the correct form in brackets.
She decided ______ (to go / going) to the library.
They enjoy ______ (to play / playing) basketball.
I want ______ (to learn / learning) a new language.
He finished ______ (to write / writing) his report.
Lesson 2:  Present Simple + Adverbs
What it means and why it matters

We use the present simple to talk about things we do regularly. Adverbs of frequency (how often) and manner (how) make these sentences richer. Instead of “I check the mail,” we can say “I daily check the mail” or “I check the mail clearly.”
How to form it
For he, she, it, add -s to the verb: “He checks the mail.”
Adverbs of frequency (usually, sometimes, often, rarely, never) usually go before the main verb: “I usually check the mail.”
With the verb to be, the adverb goes after: “She is always happy.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “He check the mail.”  ✗    Fix: “He checks the mail.”  ✓
Memory tip
Adverbs of frequency are “time helpers” — they usually sit just before the action verb.
Practice 2
Fill each blank with an adverb: daily, usually, sometimes, rarely.
I ______ go to the park at the weekend, most weeks.
She ______ reads a book before bed, but not always.
They ______ play games after school, only once in a while.
He eats breakfast every single day, so he ______ eats breakfast.
Lesson 3:  Have Got and Has Got
What it means and why it matters
We use have got and has got to show that someone owns or has something. It is a friendly way to talk about what people have.
How to form it
Use have got with I, you, we, they: “We have got a fair system.”
Use has got with he, she, it: “She has got the skills she needs.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “He have got a dog.”  ✗    Fix: “He has got a dog.”  ✓
Memory tip
have is for teams (I, you, we, they). has is for a single player (he, she, it).
Practice 3
Write have got or has got.
I ______ a new book.
She ______ a beautiful garden.
They ______ a great idea.
He ______ a friendly dog.
Lesson 4:  Wh- Questions
What it means and why it matters
Wh- questions start with who, what, where, when, why, or how. They help us ask for exact information, so we can learn about the world and about other people.
How to form it
Wh- word + helping verb + subject + main verb: “What do you master?”
Match the helping verb to the subject: use does for he/she/it. “What does she like?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “What do she like?”  ✗    Fix: “What does she like?”  ✓
Memory tip
Question Word First! Pick the right helper: what for things, who for people, where for places, when for time, why for reasons, how for the way.
Practice 4
Fill in the correct Wh- word.
______ is your favourite colour?
______ do you like to do after school?
______ does the sun set — in the morning or the evening?
______ is your best friend?
Lesson 5:  Imperatives
What it means and why it matters
Imperatives are command sentences. We use them to give instructions, directions, or requests, like “Close the door” or “Maintain your effort!” They help us tell someone clearly what to do.
How to form it
Start with the base verb and leave out “you”: “Close the window.”
For a negative command, start with Don't: “Don't run in the hallway.”
Add please to be polite: “Please wash your hands.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “Going to your seat!”  ✗    Fix: Use the base verb: “Go to your seat.”  ✓
Memory tip
Imperatives are “commanding verbs” — the verb jumps to the very front of the sentence.
Practice 5
Write the correct imperative form of the verb in brackets.
______ (close) the window, please.
______ (not run) in the hallway.
______ (finish) your homework before playing.
______ (help) your brother with his project.
Lesson 6:  Past Simple (Review)
What it means and why it matters
We use the past simple to talk about things that already happened. It lets us tell stories and describe what we did yesterday or last week.
How to form it
Regular verbs: add -ed. “play” → “played”; “walk” → “walked”.
Irregular verbs: learn them by heart. “go” → “went”; “leave” → “left”; “see” → “saw”; “eat” → “ate”.
A common mistake
Mistake: “I goed to the store.”  ✗    Fix: “I went to the store.”  ✓
Memory tip
For regular verbs: “Add -ed, and you're set!” For irregular verbs, make a list and practise them often.
Practice 6
Write the past simple of the verb in brackets.
Yesterday, I ______ (play) outside.
They ______ (gain) speed quickly during the race.
She ______ (leave) her bike at the park.
He ______ (see) a rainbow after the rain.
Lesson 7:  Conjunctions
What it means and why it matters
Conjunctions are joining words. They connect ideas so our sentences flow. Instead of “I write mail. I maintain records,” we can say “I write mail and maintain records.”
How to form it
and — to add: “I read and write.”
but — to show a difference: “I like pizza, but I don't like broccoli.”
or — to show a choice: “Would you like cake or ice cream?”
because — to give a reason: “She runs fast because she has a lot of speed.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “I wanted to go outside, and it was raining.”  ✗    Fix: These ideas are opposite, so use but: “I wanted to go outside, but it was raining.”  ✓
Memory tip
Conjunctions are like glue. Remember: “and adds, but contrasts!”
Practice 7
Fill in with and, but, or, or because.
I wanted to go to the park, ______ it started to rain.
Would you like to play football ______ basketball?
She studies hard ______ she wants to get good grades.
I like to read books, ______ I also enjoy stories on the radio.
Lesson 8:  Interview Questions
What it means and why it matters
Interview questions are questions we ask to learn about a person, their work, and their ideas — just like Asha interviews Mr. Mzee in the story. Good questions help us understand others.
How to form it
Start with a question word (What, When, Where, Who, Why, How).
Add a helping verb (do, does, is, can), then the subject, then more information: “How do you gain peace?”
Sometimes no helping verb is needed if the Wh- word is the subject: “Who helps you at work?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “When you leave?”  ✗    Fix: Add the helping verb: “When do you leave?”  ✓
Memory tip
Question Word First, then the helping verb. Keep the order steady and your questions stay clear.
Practice 8
Add the correct question word to complete each interview question.
______ do you like to eat?
______ is your favourite subject?
______ do you usually leave for school?
______ can you ride a bike?
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Verb Patterns)
1. to go   2. playing   3. to learn   4. writing
Practice 2 (Present Simple + Adverbs)
1. usually   2. sometimes   3. rarely   4. daily
Practice 3 (Have Got / Has Got)
1. have got   2. has got   3. have got   4. has got
Practice 4 (Wh- Questions)
1. What   2. What   3. When   4. Who
Practice 5 (Imperatives)
1. Close   2. Don't run   3. Finish   4. Help
Practice 6 (Past Simple)
1. played   2. gained   3. left   4. saw
Practice 7 (Conjunctions)
1. but   2. or   3. because   4. and
Practice 8 (Interview Questions)
1. What   2. What   3. When   4. How

paragraphs: Unit 1: Daily Life & Communication
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Year 4 English! In this unit you will explore daily life and communication — the things people do every day and the ways we share ideas with one another. You will read about routines, letters and mail, working hard, and being fair. You will learn how people communicate in different languages, and why steady effort helps us gain new skills. By the end, you will be able to interview someone about their daily work, write clearly about your own routine, and use new grammar to make your sentences stronger — just like Asha does in this unit's story.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Talk and write about your daily routine and the things you do every day.
Explain why communication — letters, mail, and language — matters to a community.
Use verb patterns correctly (for example, “She agrees to send the mail”).
Add adverbs to the present simple to say how and how often you do things.
Use have got and has got to talk about what people own or have.
Ask clear Wh- questions and interview questions to find out information.
Give instructions using imperatives, and join ideas with conjunctions.
Interview a person about their work and write a fair, clear report about them.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a small group of words each day.
Work through the mini-lessons in the Grammar file, and check your answers.
Read the Story, “Fair Effort, Daily Gains”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see how much you have learned.
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Reading 1 — A Day at the Posthouse
Read this short text:

Mr. Mzee works at the posthouse in his village. Every day he sorts the mail, helps people send letters, and reads letters aloud for those who cannot read well. “Not everyone has a phone,” he says. “A letter can bring peace and make someone feel remembered.” Mr. Mzee speaks two languages, so he can help almost everyone in town. His work is not fast, but he does it with effort and care. “Some days are busy, some are quiet,” he says, “but every day matters.”
After reading, answer:
What are three things Mr. Mzee does every day?
Why does he say a letter can bring peace?
Why is it helpful that Mr. Mzee speaks two languages?
Find one word in the text that means “hard work you put into something.”
Reading 2 — My Daily Routine
Read Asha's short diary:

Every morning I help my mother sweep the floor and boil the water. Then I walk to school with my friends. I usually arrive early. In class we have a daily reading time, which I really like. After school I do my chores and my homework. I try to keep a good balance between my work and my free time. If I use my time well, I can do it all.
After reading, answer:
What does Asha do before school?
What does she do after school?
What does she mean by “a good balance”?
How is Asha's daily routine the same as, or different from, yours?
Listening — An Interview (script)
Ask your AI tutor to read this aloud, taking turns as Asha and Mr. Mzee. Then read it yourself.
Asha:  “May I interview you for my school report?”
Mr. Mzee:  “Of course. That sounds like a good project.”
Asha:  “What do you do in your daily work?”
Mr. Mzee:  “I sort mail, help people send letters, and talk to visitors.”
Asha:  “Why is mail still important?”
Mr. Mzee:  “Because not everyone has a phone. A letter can make someone feel remembered.”
After listening, answer:
Which questions does Asha ask? Do they start with a Wh- word? (See Grammar Lesson 4.)
What helping words does Asha use to make her questions clear? (See Grammar Lesson 8.)
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — Describe Your Daily Routine
Get ready to talk for one minute about your day. Use these sentence starters:
“Every morning I ______.”
“I usually ______ before school.”
“After school I ______, and then I ______.”
“I try to keep a balance between ______ and ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you use at least two adverbs (daily, usually, really, clearly)? Did you speak clearly enough to understand?
Speaking 2 — Interview a Family Member
Interview someone at home about their daily work, the way Asha interviewed Mr. Mzee. Ask:
“What do you do in your daily work?”
“Why is your work important?”
“What languages do you speak?”
“What helps you keep going when the work is hard?”
Speaking Game — Give the Instructions
Play with your AI tutor or your family, using imperatives (command sentences):
Think of a simple task, like making tea or tidying a room.
Give the steps as commands: “First, boil the water. Next, add the tea. Then, wait two minutes.”
The other person listens and repeats the steps back to you.
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — An Interview Report
Interview a person about their daily work (from Speaking 2). Then write a short, fair report about them, like Asha's report about Mr. Mzee. Include:
Who the person is and what they do.
Three things they do in their daily work.
One thing you learned from them.
Writing 2 — My Daily Life

Write a paragraph called “My Daily Life”. Use at least three adverbs (daily, usually, really, clearly) and join some ideas with and, but, or because. Finish with one sentence about how you keep a good balance.
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Activity 1 — True or False
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
“Daily” means something that happens once a week.
To “maintain” something means to keep it in good condition.
A “citizen” is a person who belongs to a country.
To “cancel” something means to make it happen.
Activity 2 — Choose the Verb Pattern
Choose the correct word in brackets.
She agrees ______ (to send / sending) the mail.
They enjoy ______ (to play / playing) football after school.
He wants ______ (to learn / learning) a new language.
Activity 3 — Fill in the Adverb
Fill each blank with an adverb: daily, usually, really, clearly.
I ______ check the mail every morning.
She spoke ______ so everyone could hear her.
I ______ enjoy our reading time in class.
We ______ eat lunch at noon.
Activity 4 — Join the Sentences
Join each pair with and, but, or because.
I write the mail. I maintain the records.
I wanted to play outside. It started to rain.
She runs fast. She has a lot of speed.
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand this grammar. Can you explain it a different way?”
Practise speaking: “Can you interview me, and check how I answer?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my writing and tell me one thing to improve?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me five more sentences about verb patterns?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can talk and write about my daily routine.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use verb patterns like “agree to send”.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can add adverbs to say how and how often.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use “have got” and “has got”.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can ask clear Wh- and interview questions.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can give instructions and join ideas with conjunctions.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can interview someone and write a fair report.   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 2
Our next unit is Unit 2: Nature & Weather. Get ready:
Make sure you know all the Unit 1 words on your Word Wall.
Start noticing the weather each day, and the plants and animals around you.
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 2? Can you get me ready for Nature & Weather?”
Well done — like Asha, remember that steady daily effort helps you gain new skills. Be patient, and celebrate every new word you learn! 🌟

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 1: Daily Life & Communication
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “Fair Effort, Daily Gains”
Fair Effort, Daily Gains

Every morning, Asha helped her mother sweep the floor and boil the water before school. She liked to stay busy, because she believed that small tasks showed big effort. She also tried to work at speed, so that she still had time to read.
At school, the head teacher pinned up a notice. “Writing Contest!” it said. “Write a report about someone in your community. The winner gets a dictionary!”

Asha smiled. She wanted to improve her language and master new words, and a dictionary would help her learn faster. But she still had homework, chores, and her daily reading. “I must find a balance,” she told herself. “If I use my time well, I can do it all.”
That evening, her mother asked, “Can you manage this contest and your schoolwork?”
“I think so,” said Asha. “I will try my best and continue learning.”

She thought about who to write about. There were many important people in her village, but one stood out — Mr. Mzee, the kind citizen who worked at the posthouse. He sorted and delivered the mail every day.
After class the next day, Asha went to visit him. “Mr. Mzee,” she asked, “may I interview you for my school report?”
“Of course,” he smiled. “That sounds like a good project.”
“What do you do in your daily work?” she asked.
“I sort mail, help people send letters, and talk to visitors. I also read letters for people who cannot read well.”
“Why is mail still important?” asked Asha.
“Because not everyone has a phone. A letter can bring peace, and it can make someone feel remembered.”
Asha asked if he spoke more than one language. “Yes,” he said. “I had to master English and Kiswahili. Some people speak local languages too. It is necessary, so that I can help everyone.”
“And do you do this job every day?” Asha asked.
“Yes,” he said. “It is my usual work. Some days are busy and some are quiet, but every day matters.”
Back home, Asha began her report. She wanted it to be fair and honest, so she added all the details he had told her. She checked her spelling and used her neatest handwriting.
Her father saw her writing. “Take your time,” he said gently. “Do not leave out anything important.”
“I won't,” she replied. “This report has a purpose.”
The next morning it rained hard. A student shouted, “The contest is cancelled!” But the teacher said, “Don't worry! We will hold it in the library. Bring your reports!”
The students sat in a circle and took turns reading. Some were nervous. Others forgot their lines. Asha waited quietly. When it was her turn, she stood up and read in a clear voice:
“Mr. Mzee is a helpful citizen who delivers the mail and helps others read. He speaks two languages and works with care. He told me that daily effort helps us gain trust and knowledge.”
After she finished, one judge asked, “What did you learn from this?”
“I learned to listen, to write clearly, and to keep going even when it rains,” Asha said with a smile.

Later that day, the teacher made the announcement. “The winner is Asha! Her report was honest, clear, and full of meaning.” The whole class clapped, and Asha stood proudly, holding her prize — a brand-new dictionary.
At home, her mother hugged her. “You did it!”

“Yes,” said Asha. “I did not stop. I worked hard, and I found a good balance.” She opened her new dictionary and pointed to a word. “Look — ‘Effort: work done with care.’ Now I know the meaning in my heart.”
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
What does Asha do every morning before school?
What is the prize in the writing contest?
Who does Asha choose to write about, and what is his job?
Name three things Mr. Mzee does in his daily work.
Why does Mr. Mzee say that mail is still important?
What happened on the morning of the contest, and how did the teacher solve the problem?
Why do you think Asha's report won?
Think and talk
Asha said, “I must find a balance.” How do you keep a balance between your work and your free time?
Mr. Mzee said, “Every day matters.” What is one small thing you do daily that makes a difference?
How is Asha's daily life the same as, or different from, your own?
Find the words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 1 words and say the sentence they are in:
effort
speed
language
master
balance
citizen
mail
peace
necessary
fair
cancel
gain
Now You Try:  Writing
Write three sentences about your own daily routine. Start with: “Every morning I ______.”
Imagine you interview someone in your community. Write two questions you would ask them, and the answers you think they would give.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 2: Nature & Weather
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Eight grammar lessons

This unit has eight small grammar lessons. For each one: read what it means, look at how to form it, study the mistake to avoid (✗ → ✓), remember the tip, and then try the practice. Check your answers in the Answer Key at the end. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1:  Comparative Adjectives
What it means and why it matters

Comparative adjectives compare two things. They tell us how one thing is more or less than another — bigger, deeper, stronger. They help us describe our world clearly. For example: “A hurricane is stronger than a storm.”
How to form it
Short word (one syllable): add -er.  strong → stronger, deep → deeper.
Word ending in -y: change y to i, add -er.  happy → happier, foggy → foggier.
Long word (two or more syllables): use more before it.  beautiful → more beautiful.
Always use than to join the two things you compare.
Watch out for irregulars: good → better, bad → worse.
A common mistake
Mistake: “A hurricane is strong than a storm.”  ✗
Fix: Use the comparative form and “than”: “A hurricane is stronger than a storm.”  ✓
Memory tip
“-er for one syllable, more for two or more!” Think of the word than as a little bridge that joins the two things you are comparing.
Practice 1
Write the comparative form of the word in brackets.
An elephant is ______ (big) than a dog.
This test is ______ (easy) than the last one.
Mount Kenya is ______ (high) than that hill.
The canyon is ______ (deep) than the meadow.
Lesson 2:  Superlative Adjectives
What it means and why it matters
Superlative adjectives compare three or more things. They show that something is the most or the greatest of all. For example: “The volcano is the most powerful natural event.”
How to form it
Short word (one syllable): add -est.  tall → tallest, deep → deepest.
Word ending in -y: change y to i, add -est.  foggy → foggiest.
Long word (two or more syllables): use most before it.  exciting → most exciting.
Always put the before a superlative:  the tallest, the foggiest.
Irregulars: good → best, bad → worst.
A common mistake
Mistake: “This is most foggy morning.”  ✗
Fix: Add “the”, and use the right form: “This is the foggiest morning.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think “super means the best!” A superlative always needs its friend the standing in front of it.
Practice 2
Write the superlative form of the word in brackets. (Remember “the”.)
This is ______ (funny) film I have ever seen.
She is ______ (smart) student in our class.
That was ______ (exciting) match of the season.
This is ______ (foggy) morning of the year.
Lesson 3:  Present Continuous
What it means and why it matters
The present continuous describes an action happening right now. It makes your speaking lively: you can say exactly what is going on this moment. For example: “The deer is roaming the meadow.”
How to form it
Use the correct form of to be + verb + -ing.
I am reading.
He / She / It is roaming.   (“The storm is coming.”)
We / You / They are watching.   (“The leaves are rusting in the rain.”)
Some verbs are not usually used this way (stative verbs): know, like, understand. We say “I know the answer”, not “I am knowing”.
A common mistake
Mistake: “He is play football.”  ✗
Fix: Add -ing to the action word: “He is playing football.”  ✓
Memory tip
Picture a photo of the action happening now — someone running or dancing. If you can see it happening, use am / is / are + -ing.
Practice 3
Write the present continuous form of the verb in brackets.
The dog ______ (bark) at the gate.
I ______ (eat) my lunch.
They ______ (play) in the park.
The rain ______ (fall) on the roof.
Lesson 4:  Present Simple (Facts)
What it means and why it matters
The present simple is used for facts, habits and things that are always true — like science facts. For example: “Solar energy comes from the sun.”  “Fur keeps animals warm.”
How to form it
For I, you, we, they → use the base verb:  “Birds fly south.”
For he, she, it → add -s or -es:  “It rains.”  “She watches the sky.”
Negatives use do not / does not:  “Ice does not stay hard in the sun.”
Questions start with do / does:  “Does hail fall from clouds?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “Fur keep animals warm.”  ✗
Fix: With it (fur), add -s: “Fur keeps animals warm.”  ✓
Memory tip
“He, she, it — add -s to fit!” When the subject is one person or thing, the verb gets its little -s.
Practice 4
Write the correct present simple form of the verb in brackets.
The Earth ______ (move) around the sun.
Cats ______ (chase) mice.
The sun ______ (rise) in the east.
Solar power ______ (come) from the sun.
Lesson 5:  The Modal Verb “can”
What it means and why it matters
Can is a special helping verb. It tells us what is possible or what someone is able to do. For example: “A tornado can destroy houses.”  “You can see moisture on leaves.”
How to form it
Subject + can + base verb (no -s, -ed or -ing).
Yes: “I can swim.”
No: “I cannot (can't) swim.”
Question: “Can you swim?”  (Swap the order.)
Good news: can never changes. It stays “can” for I, you, he, she, it, we and they.
A common mistake
Mistake: “He cans play guitar.”  ✗    Also: “I can playing.”  ✗
Fix: “He can play guitar.”  ✓   Keep the base verb after “can”.
Memory tip
Think of can as a little superhero: it gives the verb the power to happen — and superheroes never change their name!
Practice 5
Complete each sentence with can + the verb in brackets.
I ______ (dance) very well.
They ______ (finish) their homework on time.
She ______ (see) the stars at night.
A storm ______ (damage) crops.
Lesson 6:  Wh- Questions
What it means and why it matters
Wh- questions start with a wh- word and ask for information. Good learners ask lots of them! For example: “What causes a hurricane?”  “Where does hail form?”
How to form it
The wh- words: what, where, when, why, who, how.
Order: wh- word + helping verb + subject + verb.  “Where does hail form?”
Match the word to the answer: where for a place, when for a time, why for a reason.
A common mistake
Mistake: “You are going where?”  ✗
Fix: Put the wh- word first and swap the order: “Where are you going?”  ✓
Memory tip
Call them your “wonder words” — what, where, when, why, who and how are the keys that unlock new information about the world.
Practice 6
Write the correct wh- word to complete each question.
______ causes rainbows?
______ do you go to school?
______ is your best friend?
______ do you feel today?
Lesson 7:  Prepositions of Place
What it means and why it matters
Prepositions of place tell us where something is. They help us describe positions clearly. For example: “The moss grows on the surface.”  “We stayed inside the hall.”
How to form it
Choose the preposition that shows the position between two things:
on — resting on a surface:  “The bird is on the roof.”
under — below something:  “The rabbit hides under a bush.”
in / inside — within a space:  “We are inside the hall.”
next to — beside:  “The lamp is next to the sofa.”
between — in the middle of two:  “The ball is between the chairs.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “The cat is in the table.”  ✗
Fix: Use on for resting on a surface: “The cat is on the table.”  ✓
Memory tip
Picture a map of your room. Say where each thing sits: “The bed is in the corner, the rug is under the chair.” The little location word is the preposition.
Practice 7
Choose the correct preposition:  on · under · in · next to · between
The cat is ______ the box. (it is inside)
The toy is ______ the table. (resting on top)
The chair is ______ the desk. (beside it)
The dog is lying ______ the tree. (below the branches)
Lesson 8:  Imperatives
What it means and why it matters
Imperatives give a command, instruction or request. They tell someone what to do. For example: “Observe the storm safely!”  “Move inside!”
How to form it
Use the base verb, with no subject:  “Go.”  “Listen.”  (We do not say “You go.”)
To say “do not”, add don't:  “Don't run!”
To be polite, add please:  “Please sit down.”
To include yourself, use Let's:  “Let's move inside.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “You listen to me.”  ✗
Fix: Drop the “you” — it is already understood: “Listen to me.”  ✓
Memory tip
Remember: “No ‘you’, just the action!” An imperative is a friendly nudge that jumps straight to the verb.
Practice 8
Write the correct imperative form of the verb in brackets.
______ (close) the door, please.
______ (not touch) that!
______ (help) your sister with her homework.
______ (listen) carefully to the instructions.
Answer Key
Practice 1 — Comparative Adjectives
1. bigger   2. easier   3. higher   4. deeper
Practice 2 — Superlative Adjectives
1. the funniest   2. the smartest   3. the most exciting   4. the foggiest
Practice 3 — Present Continuous
1. is barking   2. am eating   3. are playing   4. is falling
Practice 4 — Present Simple (Facts)
1. moves   2. chase   3. rises   4. comes
Practice 5 — Modal Verb “can”
1. can dance   2. can finish   3. can see   4. can damage
Practice 6 — Wh- Questions
1. What   2. Where   3. Who   4. How
Practice 7 — Prepositions of Place
1. in   2. on   3. next to   4. under
Practice 8 — Imperatives
1. Close   2. Don't touch   3. Help   4. Listen

paragraphs: Unit 2: Nature & Weather
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 2! In this unit you will explore the natural world and the weather. You will learn to name places in nature — canyons, meadows and bays — and to talk about weather, from a foggy morning to a powerful hurricane. You will compare things (which storm is stronger?), describe facts about the world, and ask questions like a real scientist. Along the way you will read about weather, listen to a weather report, speak about the world around you, and write your own nature facts and poems. By the end, you will be ready to explain how weather works and how to stay safe — just like Mosi and Leila do in this unit's story.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Name and describe places in nature and different kinds of weather.
Compare two things using comparative adjectives (deeper, stronger, foggier).
Say which is the greatest using superlative adjectives (the deepest, the most powerful).
Describe what is happening now using the present continuous (“The storm is coming.”).
State facts about nature using the present simple (“Solar energy comes from the sun.”).
Say what is possible using can (“A tornado can destroy houses.”).
Ask and answer wh- questions about the weather.
Say where things are using prepositions of place, and give instructions with imperatives.
Explain simple ways to stay safe in a storm.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few weather and nature words each day.
Work through the eight short lessons in the Grammar file, doing the practice for each.
Read the Story, “The Storm and the Science Tent”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Reading 1 — Weather Around the World
Read this short text:

Weather is different all around the world. In some places, the air is foggy and grey in the morning. In other places, a hot sun makes people sweat. Rain helps plants grow, because plants need moisture. A gentle storm brings rain, but a hurricane brings very strong wind. Weather can be calm, and weather can be wild. Wherever we live, we watch the sky and learn.
After reading, answer:
Name three kinds of weather in the text.
Why do plants need moisture?
What is the difference between a gentle storm and a hurricane, in the text?
What is the weather like where you live today?
Reading 2 — A Poem: “The Foggy Morning”
The morning air is damp and grey,
The hills have almost gone away.
So still, so quiet, soft and slow —
The whole world hides in fog below.
After reading, answer:
Which words tell you how the foggy morning feels and looks? (Find three.)
Why does the poet say the hills “have almost gone away”?
How does a foggy morning make you feel? (You will write your own weather poem later.)
Listening 1 — The Weather Report
Listen to this weather report. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)

“Good morning! Here is today's weather. It is foggy near the lake, so please walk slowly. Later, the sun will come out, and it will be warm. Tonight, a storm may bring rain and hail. Remember: if you hear thunder, go inside and stay calm. Stay safe, and enjoy your day!”
What is the weather like near the lake in the morning?
What may the storm bring tonight?
What should you do if you hear thunder? Why?
Listening 2 — Two Friends at the Science Fair
Listen to Mosi and Leila talking. (Ask your AI tutor to read the two parts.)
Mosi: “Look at our poster! A tornado spins faster than any other storm.”
Leila: “Yes, and a hurricane is stronger than a normal storm. The volcano is the most powerful of all!”
Which storm spins the fastest, according to Mosi?
Leila uses the words “stronger” and “most powerful.” Which one compares two things, and which one compares many? (See Grammar Lessons 1 and 2.)
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — Describe Today's Weather
Look outside, then get ready to say:
“Today the weather is ______.”  (foggy / sunny / rainy / windy)
“The sky looks ______.”
“It is a good day for ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you speak clearly? Did you use at least one weather word from this unit?
Speaking 2 — Compare Two Things in Nature
Practise comparing, using comparative adjectives:
“A canyon is deeper than a meadow.”
“A hurricane is stronger than a storm.”
“Today is foggier than yesterday.”
Now make three sentences of your own comparing two things you can see.
Speaking Game — Guess the Weather
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person thinks of a kind of weather but keeps it secret.
The others ask questions: “Is it hot?” “Can you see far?” “Does it make a loud noise?”
Keep asking until someone guesses: “Is it a storm?” Then swap turns.
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — A Weather Fact File

Choose one kind of powerful weather: a hurricane, a tornado, or a volcano. Write three or four fact sentences about it. Use the present simple for facts. For example: “A hurricane brings strong wind. It can knock down trees.”
Writing 2 — Your Own Weather Poem
Write your own short poem called “The ______ Morning” (snowy, sunny, rainy, stormy). Use describing words for how it looks, sounds and feels, like the poem you read:
The morning air is ______ and ______, / The ______ have almost gone away. / ______, ______, ______ and ______ — / The whole world ______ below.
Then draw a picture to go with your poem.
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Activity 1 — True or False (Nature Facts)
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
A canyon is deeper than a meadow.
Solar energy comes from the moon.
Hail is made of small balls of ice.
A gentle storm is stronger than a hurricane.
Answers: 1. True   2. False (it comes from the sun)   3. True   4. False (a hurricane is stronger)
Activity 2 — Match the Word to the Meaning
Match each word to its meaning. Write the pairs (for example, 1–c).
1. meadow      a. small balls of ice that fall in a storm
2. hail        b. a large field of grass and flowers
3. moisture    c. the soft hair that covers some animals
4. fur         d. a small amount of water in the air
Answers: 1–b   2–a   3–d   4–c
Activity 3 — Fill in the Blanks (Weather Around You)
Today the weather is ______.
The kind of weather I like best is ______, because ______.
In a storm, the safest thing to do is ______.
One animal that has thick fur is the ______.
These are about you, so there is no single right answer — check your sentences with your AI tutor.
Activity 4 — Ask a Scientist (Wh- Questions)
Write the wh- question for each answer.
______________________?  →  “A hurricane is caused by warm water and strong wind.”
______________________?  →  “Hail forms in the clouds during a storm.”
______________________?  →  “Solar power comes from the sun.”
Example answers: 1. What causes a hurricane?   2. Where does hail form?   3. Where does solar power come from? (Your wording may be a little different — check with your tutor.)
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “How do I say this word? Can you listen and help me?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my writing and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more comparative sentences about the weather?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can name and describe kinds of weather and places in nature.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can compare two things (deeper, stronger, foggier).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can say which is the greatest (the deepest, the most powerful).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can say what is happening now (“The storm is coming.”).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can state a fact about nature (“Fur keeps animals warm.”).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can ask and answer wh- questions about the weather.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can explain a simple way to stay safe in a storm.   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 3
Our next unit is Unit 3: Food & Health. You will learn to talk about food, meals, and staying healthy and strong.
Make sure you know all the Unit 2 words on your Word Wall.
Start noticing food words in English at home — the names of fruits, vegetables and meals.
Keep using English every day, even for a few minutes.
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 3? Can you get me ready?”
Well done — you have learned so much about the natural world! Be patient with yourself, keep practising, and celebrate every new word you learn. 🌟

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 2: Nature & Weather
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Storm and the Science Tent”
The Storm and the Science Tent

Every year, Mosi looked forward to the village science fair. His school set up a big tent near the lake, where students shared their projects with the whole community. This year, Mosi and his friend Leila had planned a weather display. They wanted to teach people how storms work and how to stay safe.
On the morning of the fair, the sky already looked strange. A grey, foggy mist covered the path. “I can barely see the ground,” Leila said. “We should walk slowly.”
Mosi nodded. “It's the foggiest morning we've had all year.”

When they reached the lake, the tent was ready. Their teacher, Mr Duma, was arranging the students' posters. “Set up your display near the solar light,” he said. “The sun isn't strong today, but it will still help.”
Leila unrolled their poster about hurricanes, volcanoes and tornadoes. “This one shows a tornado moving across a field,” she said. “It spins faster than any other storm!”
Mosi added, “And this picture shows a volcano with hot lava. It is dangerous, but it is also beautiful.”
People began to arrive. Some students talked about meadows, others about the lake or the forest. A younger boy pointed at Mosi's display. “What is a canyon?” he asked.
“It's a deep valley,” Mosi said. “Much deeper than a meadow. A river can run through it. A canyon can be the deepest land on Earth.”
Mr Duma clapped his hands. “Time for a safety quiz! Leila, your turn.”
Leila stood up. “What causes a hurricane?” she asked.
A girl raised her hand. “Warm water and strong wind?”
“Correct!” Leila smiled.
“Now Mosi,” said the teacher.
Mosi pointed to a picture. “Where does hail fall from?”
“From the clouds during a storm,” a student answered.
“Right,” said Mosi. “And hail can damage crops, so we must always watch the weather.”
Just then, the wind picked up. The surface of the lake began to ripple. A few parents looked up at the sky.
Suddenly, a man shouted, “It's coming back! The storm from last week!”
Some children began to panic. “Don't run!” Mr Duma said firmly. “Let's all move inside the hall. Quickly, but calmly.”
As they packed their things, Mosi felt something cold on his cheek. “Hail!” he said. Small balls of ice bounced on the ground. “Let's go!”
They reached the hall just as the wind began to howl outside. Mr Duma closed the door. “We're safe here,” he said.
Leila sat down, catching her breath. “My heart is beating fast,” she said. Her shirt was wet with sweat.
“That was scary,” said Mosi. “But it was also kind of exciting.”
Inside, they carried on with their weather talk. They showed how animals use thick fur to stay warm in snowy weather, and how moisture in the soil helps plants grow.
“Rust forms when metal gets wet and is left too long,” Leila explained. “Like that old nail outside.”
“And mold grows on food when we leave it too long,” Mosi added. “That's why we always cover our lunch boxes!”
One girl pointed at a chart. “What's that tall tree near the bay?” she asked.
“That's a royal palm tree,” Leila said. “It grows near the water. It is tall and strong, like a king's tree.”
Mosi smiled. “Nature is powerful,” he said, “but we can learn to understand it.”
When the storm finally passed, the sky cleared. Sunshine filled the room. “Look,” a student said. “The light is shining brightly now.”
Mr Duma smiled. “You all did well today. You learned, you taught, and you stayed calm. You showed real courage.”
Mosi and Leila packed up their project. “Even with the storm, this was the most exciting science fair ever,” Mosi said.
“And we didn't even have to roam far to learn something new,” Leila laughed.
Outside, puddles sparkled in the sun. The wind had slowed — but the lesson had stayed.
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
What were Mosi and Leila planning to teach people at the science fair?
What was the weather like on the morning of the fair?
In the safety quiz, what causes a hurricane, and where does hail fall from?
Why did the teacher tell everyone to walk, not run, to the hall?
Name two things Mosi and Leila taught about nature once they were safe inside.
Why did Mosi say the fair was “the most exciting science fair ever”?
Think and talk
How did the children feel when the storm came back? How did staying calm help them?
Mr Duma said the children “showed real courage.” What does courage mean to you?
The story happens by a lake in East Africa. What weather do you have where you live? Is it like the story or different?
Find the words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 2 words and read out the sentence each one is in:
foggy
solar
tornado
canyon
hail
surface
breath
sweat
fur
moisture
rust
mold
royal
roam
Now You Try:  Writing
Write three or four sentences about a time you saw strong weather — a storm, heavy rain, or a very hot day. Try to use at least two Unit 2 words.

Imagine you have a display at the science fair. Draw your poster and write two facts about the weather on it. Use one comparative (like “stronger than”) and one fact sentence (like “Solar power comes from the sun.”).
Finish this sentence in your own words and explain why: “In a storm, the safest thing to do is ______.”

paragraphs: Vocabulary  —  Unit 2: Nature & Weather
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-study word book

These are the important words for Unit 2. You will meet them in your Lesson, your Grammar file, and in the story “The Storm and the Science Tent”. Learn a few words each day: read the meaning, say the example out loud, then do the quick practice. Your AI tutor can check your sentences and help you say each word correctly.
Group 1: Water, Air and the Body
Practise these words
Take a slow, deep breath. Then say: “When I run fast, I make a lot of sweat.”
Touch something in your home and say: “The surface of this ______ is smooth / rough.”
Name one animal with fur and say why the fur helps it.
Group 2: Places in Nature
Practise these words
Say which is deeper: a canyon or a meadow? Use the word “deeper”.
Draw a bay with land around the water. Label it in English.
Finish: “I would like to visit a ______ because ______.”
Group 3: Weather and Storms
Practise these words
Put these in order from smallest to biggest storm: hail, storm, hurricane. Talk it over with your tutor.
Look outside. Say one sentence about today's weather using foggy, snowy, or storm.
Finish: “In a big storm, you should ______ to stay safe.”
Group 4: How Nature Works
Practise these words
Say one fact: “Solar power comes from the ______.”
Name one thing that can get rust and one thing that can grow mold.
Finish: “The animals roam across the ______.”
My Word Wall

A soft, fuzzy growth that forms on old food or wet things.

To move around freely with no set place to go.
Lions roam across the grasslands looking for food.
Grand or special, or to do with kings and queens.
The royal palm tree grows tall and strong near the bay.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Why does metal get rust? Why does food grow mold?”
“Can you use each of these words in a new sentence for me?”

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 3: Food and Health
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Six grammar lessons

This unit has six short grammar lessons. For each one: read what it means and why it matters, look at how to form it, study the common mistake (✗ → ✓), remember the tip, and then try the practice. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it! Check your answers in the Answer Key at the end.
Lesson 1:  Modal Verbs — must and might
What it means and why it matters

Modal verbs are special helping verbs. In this unit we use two of them: must and might. Must shows that something is necessary or very important — a rule. Might shows that something is possible, but not certain. Knowing both helps you explain rules and possibilities clearly.
How to form it
must  →  subject + must + base verb:  “You must wash fresh fruit.”
might  →  subject + might + base verb:  “Pesticides might harm plants.”
Negatives: mustn't means “not allowed” (“You mustn't eat old meat.”). might not means “probably won't” (“She might not come today.”).
A common mistake
Mistake: “You must to wash your hands.”  ✗
Fix: After must or might, use the base verb with no “to”: “You must wash your hands.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of must as a strong rule from a teacher (“You must!”), and might as a gentle “maybe” from a friend (“We might…”).
Practice 1
Complete each sentence with must or might:
You ______ finish your lunch before going outside. (a rule)
It ______ rain tomorrow, so bring a jacket! (a possibility)
We ______ wash the vegetables before we cook them. (a rule)
She ______ go to the market later if she has time. (a possibility)
Lesson 2:  Yes/No Questions
What it means and why it matters
A Yes/No question is a question that can be answered with “yes” or “no”. It helps you get clear, direct information — for example, “Is this meat fresh?” → “Yes, it is.”
How to form it
Start with a helping verb (is, are, do, does, can), then the subject, then the rest:
Is alcohol bad for health?
Does rice grow in water?
Are you hungry?
Can we buy bread at the bakery?
Use do with I, you, we, they. Use does with he, she, it.
A common mistake
Mistake: “Does he eats breakfast?”  ✗
Fix: After does, use the base verb with no “s”: “Does he eat breakfast?”  ✓
Memory tip
Remember the order: Helping verb + subject + the rest. Say it like a little rhyme.
Practice 2
Choose the correct helping verb (Do, Does, Are, Is) to start each question:
______ cats eat fish?
______ your brother like rice?
______ they hungry now?
______ it raining outside?
______ you want more soup?
Lesson 3:  The Infinitive of Purpose
What it means and why it matters
The infinitive of purpose explains why we do something. When we say, “We eat vitamins to stay healthy,” the words “to stay healthy” tell us the reason. It makes your sentences clear and meaningful.
How to form it
Use to + the base verb to give the reason:
Action + purpose:  “Farmers work to grow crops.”
“We eat good food to help our brain and body.”
“She saves money to buy fresh food.”
You can also say so that with a subject: “We wash food so that we do not get sick.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “We eat vitamins staying healthy.”  ✗   (no “to”, wrong verb form)
Fix: Use to + the base verb: “We eat vitamins to stay healthy.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of to as a little bridge that joins the action to the reason. Cross the bridge with “to” + a verb.
Practice 3
Complete each sentence with “to” + the verb in brackets:
He exercises every day ______ (keep) fit.
She saves her money ______ (buy) fresh fruit.
We wash our hands ______ (stay) clean.
They study hard ______ (improve) their English.
Lesson 4:  Imperatives (Giving Commands)
What it means and why it matters
Imperatives are verbs used to give a command, an instruction, or advice. They are perfect for recipes, posters, and safety rules — “Wash the fruit.” “Gather the cattle.”
How to form it
Use the base verb, and leave out the subject “you”: Gather the cattle!
Add details to be clear: Avoid deadly plants.  ·  Carefully wash the meat.
For a command not to do something, use Don't: Don't eat old food.
Be polite by adding please: Please close the door.
A common mistake
Mistake: “You gather the cattle!”  ✗   (do not include “you”)
Fix: Start with the verb: “Gather the cattle!”  ✓
Memory tip
An imperative is an action word giving a direct order — like a coach shouting one clear word: “Run!” “Stop!”
Practice 4
Write the imperative using the verb in brackets:
______ (eat) your vegetables!
______ (wash) the fruit before you eat it.
______ (not / touch) that hot pot!
______ (help) your brother carry the rice.
Lesson 5:  Comparisons
What it means and why it matters
Comparisons show how two things are the same or different. They help you share opinions and make choices — for example, “Fresh food is better than old food.”
How to form it
Short adjective (one syllable): add -er.  tall → taller,  fresh → fresher.
Adjective ending in -y: change y to -ier.  happy → happier,  healthy → healthier.
Long adjective (three or more syllables): use more.  delicious → more delicious.
Then add than:  “Rice is cheaper than meat.”
Two special ones to remember: good → better, bad → worse.
A common mistake
Mistake: “She is more tall than her brother.”  ✗
Fix: Short adjectives add -er: “She is taller than her brother.”  ✓   Also remember than: not “This car is faster.” but “This car is faster than that one.”
Memory tip
Remember the rhyme: “For one small word, just add an -er; for a long one, use more, for sure!”
Practice 5
Write the correct comparison word:
This mango is ______ (sweet) than that one.
Fresh bread is ______ (good) than old bread.
Running is ______ (fun) than walking.
Summer is ______ (hot) than spring.
Lesson 6:  Wh- Questions
What it means and why it matters
Wh- questions start with what, who, where, when, why, or how. They help you ask for details and learn more — “Why is famine dangerous?” “How do chemicals affect food?”
How to form it
What — things:  “What do you like to eat?”
Who — people:  “Who cooked the lamb?”
Where — places:  “Where does rice grow?”
When — time:  “When is dinner?”
Why — reasons:  “Why is famine dangerous?”
How — the way/process:  “How do chemicals affect food?”
With the verb “to be”, it comes straight after the Wh- word: “Where is the bakery?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “Why he is running?”  ✗
Fix: Put the verb in the right order: “Why is he running?”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of a Wh- word as a fishing rod: each one helps you “fish” for a different kind of answer.
Practice 6
Complete each question with the correct Wh- word:
______ do you want for lunch?
______ is your favourite fruit?
______ does rice grow?
______ do we wash our food?  (Ask for the reason.)
______ do you feel today?
Answer Key
Practice 1 (must / might)
1. must   2. might   3. must   4. might
Practice 2 (Yes/No Questions)
1. Do   2. Does   3. Are   4. Is   5. Do
Practice 3 (Infinitive of Purpose)
1. to keep   2. to buy   3. to stay   4. to improve
Practice 4 (Imperatives)
1. Eat your vegetables!   2. Wash the fruit before you eat it.   3. Don't touch that hot pot!   4. Help your brother carry the rice.
Practice 5 (Comparisons)
1. sweeter   2. better   3. more fun   4. hotter
Practice 6 (Wh- Questions)
1. What   2. What   3. Where   4. Why   5. How

paragraphs: Unit 3: Food and Health
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

Food is one of Allah's great blessings. In this unit you will learn how to talk about food and health in English. You will read about where food comes from, how to keep it clean and safe, and why fresh food helps your body and your brain. You will learn to give clear instructions, ask good questions, and compare healthy and unhealthy choices. Along the way you will meet many new words — from sandwich and rice to pesticide and famine — and you will use grammar that helps you explain rules, reasons, and comparisons. By the end, you will be ready to read this unit's story, “The Bitter Lunch”, about a boy named Amin who learns to check his food with care.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Name and describe many foods, and talk about where food comes from.
Explain why fresh, clean food is good for your body and your brain.
Give clear instructions using imperatives (“Wash the fruit.”).
Use must and might to talk about rules and possibilities.
Ask Yes/No questions and Wh- questions about food and health.
Explain reasons using the infinitive of purpose (“We eat to grow.”).
Compare foods and choices (“Fresh food is better than old food.”).
Write instructions, a health poster, a short report, and a short story.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and tick your Word Wall.
Work through the six short lessons in the Grammar file, one at a time.
Read the Story file, “The Bitter Lunch”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Reading 1 — From Farm to Plate
Read this text carefully:

Every meal begins far away from your plate. Farmers work hard to grow rice in wet fields and to keep cattle in dry ones. Their labor starts early in the morning and ends when the sun goes down. When the food is ready, it travels to the market, the bakery, and finally to your home. Fresh food is full of goodness. It helps your body grow and helps your brain think clearly. But food must be kept clean. Sometimes farmers use pesticide to stop insects from eating the crops. A little can protect the plants, but too many chemicals can be harmful. That is why we must always wash fruit and vegetables before we eat them.
After reading, answer:
Name two kinds of work a farmer does in the text.
Where does food travel before it reaches your home?
Why do some farmers use pesticide? Why can too many chemicals be a problem?
What should you always do before eating fruit and vegetables? Why?
Reading 2 — A Poster on the Wall
Amin's teacher put this health poster on the classroom wall. Read it:
Eat fresh food to stay strong.  ·  Wash your hands before you eat.  ·  Drink clean water every day.  ·  Fresh fruit is better than sweets.  ·  Never eat food that smells strange.
After reading, answer:
The poster gives commands. Find two verbs that tell you what to do. (These are imperatives — see Grammar Lesson 4.)
The poster makes one comparison. Write it out. (See Grammar Lesson 5.)
Which piece of advice do you think is the most important? Say why.
Listening 1 — At the Health Center
Listen to this short talk between a nurse and a boy who feels sick. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)
Nurse:  “Good morning. How do you feel today?”
Amin:  “My stomach hurts. I ate an old sandwich last night.”
Nurse:  “Was the meat fresh?”
Amin:  “No. It was not fresh, and it smelled strange.”
Nurse:  “You must be more careful. Old food might make you very sick. Always check your food first.”
After listening, answer:
Why does Amin's stomach hurt?
Find the Yes/No question the nurse asks. (See Grammar Lesson 2.)
Find one sentence with must and one with might. What is the difference between them? (See Grammar Lesson 1.)
Listening 2 — The Market Song
Listen and join in:
“Rice and lamb and fresh green peas, gather them with care, if you please! Wash them well and cook them slow — healthy food helps children grow!”
Which foods are named in the song?
What does the song say healthy food helps children do?
The last line explains a reason. Which two words show purpose? (Hint: “to grow” — see Grammar Lesson 3.)
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — My Favourite Meal
Get ready to talk for one minute about a meal you love. Try to use these sentences:
“My favourite meal is ______.”
“It is made with ______ and ______.”
“We eat it to ______.”  (a reason — see Grammar Lesson 3)
Record yourself. Check: Did you name the food clearly? Did you give a reason? Did you speak loudly enough?
Speaking 2 — Giving Healthy Advice
Imagine your younger cousin is about to eat. Give three commands (imperatives) to help them stay healthy:
“______ your hands first.”
“______ the fruit before you eat it.”
“Don't ______ food that smells strange.”
Speaking Game — Healthy or Not?
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person names a food or a habit, for example “fresh mangoes” or “too much candy”.
The other person answers with a comparison: “Fresh mangoes are better than candy because ______.”
Swap turns. Try to use the words fresh, deadly, healthy, and better.
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — How to Make a Sandwich (Instructions)
Write four to six steps that explain how to make a healthy sandwich. Use imperatives (command verbs) and order words like first, next, then, and finally:
First, wash your hands.
Next, take two slices of fresh bread.
Finally, ______.
Writing 2 — A Health Poster
Design a poster titled “Why Fresh Food is Important”. Write four short pieces of advice. Use at least one comparison (“Fresh food is better than …”) and one sentence with must.
Writing 3 — A Short Report

Write five to six sentences for a mini-report called “How Pesticides Help and Harm”. Explain one way pesticides help farmers and one way too many chemicals can harm people. End with your own advice using must.
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Activity 1 — True or False (Food & Health Facts)
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
Fresh food is better for your body than old food.
You should never wash fruit before eating it.
A pesticide is a chemical that kills insects on plants.
Alcohol is a safe drink for children.
Activity 2 — Sort the Words
Make two lists from these words: fresh, deadly, chewy, sandwich, rice, stomach. One list is for naming words (nouns) and one is for describing words (adjectives). Check with your AI tutor.
Activity 3 — Fill in the Blanks (must / might)
Choose must or might for each sentence:
You ______ wash your hands before you eat. (a rule)
It ______ rain later, so the farmers are working fast. (a possibility)
We ______ check that meat is fresh before we cook it. (a rule)
She ______ buy bread from the bakery if it is open. (a possibility)
Activity 4 — Ask the Question
Write a Wh- question for each answer:
______________________?  →  “Rice grows in wet fields.”  (Where …)
______________________?  →  “Farmers use pesticide to protect the crops.”  (Why …)
______________________?  →  “A famine happens when there is not enough food.”  (What …)
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand pesticides. Can you explain it in a simpler way?”
Practise speaking: “How do I say this word? Can you listen and help me?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my health poster and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more must and might questions?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can name foods and say where food comes from.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can explain why fresh, clean food is good for me.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can give clear instructions using imperatives.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use must and might correctly.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can ask Yes/No questions and Wh- questions.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can compare two foods or choices.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can write instructions, a poster, and a short report.   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 4:  Community & Communication
In your next unit you will learn how people live and talk together in a community — how we help neighbours, share news, write messages, and communicate kindly. Get ready like this:
Make sure you know all the Unit 3 words on your Word Wall.
Notice the people who help your community: the baker, the farmer, the nurse, the teacher.
Keep using English every day, even for a few minutes.
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 4 about Community and Communication? Can you get me ready?”
Well done — you have learned a lot about food and health. Remember that food is a blessing: eat with care, give thanks, and share with others. 🌟

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 3: Food and Health
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Bitter Lunch”
The Bitter Lunch

In the hot fields of Sudan, Amin helped his uncle grow rice and care for the cattle. Every morning before school, he fed the cows and carried water from the well. It was hard labor, but his uncle always smiled and said, “We work hard now to eat well later.”

One day at school, Amin's teacher stood at the front of the class. “I have a project for you,” she said. “Each of you will write a story about food, and it must carry a health message. You must use our new words and write with care. You might even share your story at the community lunch.”
Amin raised his hand. “Can it be a true story?” he asked.
“Yes,” the teacher said, “as long as it teaches something helpful.”
That evening, Amin walked to the village bakery. It smelled of warm bread. He was hungry, so he bought a sandwich filled with salty meat, chewy cheese, and plenty of spice.
“I should tell you,” said the baker, “this meat is not fresh. But it still tastes good.” Amin was too hungry to care.
At home, he shared part of the sandwich with his cousin Samia. She frowned. “I think this meat is from sick cattle,” she said. “There is a strange smell.”
“You worry too much,” said Amin, and he ate the rest.
Late that night, Amin's stomach hurt badly. He felt heat in his belly and cold in his hands, and he could not sleep. Samia heard him groaning and woke her father.
At the health center, a kind nurse checked him carefully. “This may be food poisoning,” she said. “Some chemicals are used to clean old meat, and they can be dangerous if they are not washed off.”
Amin groaned. “I only ate a sandwich. It had a lot of spice, but the meat may have been old.”

“You must be more careful,” said the nurse gently. “Old meat and dirty tools can lead to disease. Eaten too often, bad food can even lead to a serious sickness like cancer. Always check your food first.”
The next day, Amin stayed home from school. His uncle brought him warm soup and said, “You are lucky this was not deadly. Always check what you eat.”
By the afternoon, Amin felt better. Then he remembered his story. He sat outside with his notebook and wrote:
“I once ate a bad sandwich. It looked fine, but it was not fresh. It gave me a sore stomach, and I learned to check what I eat.”
He thought for a while, then wrote more: “Some chemicals help to clean food, but too much is harmful. Farmers must use less pesticide, and people must wash their food with care.”
“What else should I write?” he asked Samia.
“Gather some facts about food and health,” she said. “Then give good advice.”

Amin smiled. “That helps.” He added a final part: “We eat to feed our brain and our body, and good food helps us grow. Too much alcohol or dirty food can make us sick. A famine shows us how precious food really is, and how we must protect it.”
On the day of the community lunch, Amin read his story aloud. A few children laughed at first — until he reached the part about his illness. Then the room grew quiet, and everyone listened.
“Thank you for that, Amin,” said the teacher. “Now, who else would like to share?”
Samia raised her hand. “Amin also learned where to put a comma in a list of food!” The whole room giggled.
Then the village elder stood up. “Food is a gift from Allah,” he said. “Let us not waste it, and let us never eat without checking it first.”
As the food was served — lamb, rice, and sweet dates — Amin chose only what looked clean and safe. The meal was simple, but everything was warm and fresh.
Amin leaned over to Samia. “This is the freshest lamb I have had in weeks,” he whispered.
She grinned. “That is because I helped to cook it.”
Amin took a bite and smiled. This time, his stomach did not hurt at all.
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
What work does Amin do each morning before school?
What project does the teacher give the class?
Why did the sandwich make Amin sick? What did the nurse say caused it?
What advice does the nurse give Amin about his food?
What health message does Amin put in his own story?
At the community lunch, how does Amin choose his food, and why?
Think and talk
How did Amin feel about his sandwich at the start? How did he feel by the end of the story? What changed his mind?
Samia warned Amin, but he did not listen. When is it wise to listen to someone's warning about food?
The elder said, “Food is a gift from Allah.” What are some ways your family shows thanks for food and avoids wasting it?
Find the words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 3 words and say the sentence each one is in:
rice
cattle
bakery
sandwich
fresh
chewy
spice
stomach
chemicals
pesticide
gather
brain
famine
lamb
comma
Now You Try:  Writing
Write your own short health message, like Amin's, in three or four sentences. Begin: “I learned to ______.”
Write three imperatives (command sentences) that would fit on a poster about safe, healthy food.
Draw Amin at the community lunch choosing clean, fresh food. Write one sentence under your picture about the wise choice he made.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 4: Community and Communication
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Seven grammar mini-lessons

This unit has seven small grammar lessons. Read one lesson at a time, try the practice, and then check your answers in the Answer Key at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1:  Countable and Uncountable Nouns
What it means and why it matters

Some nouns we can count one by one — one book, two books, three books. We call these countable nouns. Other nouns we cannot count as separate items — like water, rice, or information. We call these uncountable nouns. Knowing the difference helps you choose the right words, so your sentences make sense.
How to form and use it
Countable: use a number, or many / few.  “I have three apples.”  “There are many books.”
Uncountable: do not use a number. Use much, little, or some.  “I need some water.”  “We have much information.”
Use a or an only with singular countable nouns: “a book”, but never “a information”.
A common mistake
Mistake: “Can you give me some informations?”  ✗
Fix: information is uncountable, so it never adds an “s”: “Can you give me some information?”  ✓
Memory tip
If you can put a number in front and add “s” (one apple, two apples), it is countable. If you would pour it or scoop it (water, rice, sugar), it is usually uncountable.
Practice 1
Choose the correct word:
I have ______ (many / much) friends at school.
Could you give me ______ (some / many) information about the project?
There are ______ (few / little) cookies left in the jar.
I would like ______ (a / an) orange, please.
We need ______ (much / many) chairs for the party.
Lesson 2:  There is / There are
What it means and why it matters
We use there is and there are to say that something exists. This helps us describe a place and share information about what is around us.
How to form and use it
There is → one thing (singular), or an uncountable noun.  “There is a library.”  “There is water in the glass.”
There are → two or more things (plural).  “There are three scientists in the lab.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “There is two cats on the wall.”  ✗
Fix: “cats” is plural, so use there are: “There are two cats on the wall.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think: is = 1 (one single star shining). are = more than one (many stars twinkling together).
Practice 2
Choose the correct phrase:
______ a bird in the tree. (is / are)
______ three cars in the parking lot. (is / are)
______ an elephant at the zoo. (is / are)
______ many students in the classroom. (is / are)
Lesson 3:  Present Simple (Facts)
What it means and why it matters
We use the present simple to talk about facts, habits, and things that are always true — like “Mail carriers deliver letters daily.” It helps you state true information clearly.
How to form and use it
For I, you, we, they: use the base verb.  “They deliver letters.”  “I read books.”
For he, she, it: add -s or -es.  “She plays.”  “He watches.”
You can add time words: daily, often, every day, sometimes.
A common mistake
Mistake: “She play soccer every day.”  ✗
Fix: the subject is “she”, so add “s”: “She plays soccer every day.”  ✓
Memory tip
He, She, It → don't forget the “s”! For everyone else, just use the plain base verb.
Practice 3
Write the correct present simple form of the verb in brackets:
Dogs ______ (bark) when they see strangers.
She ______ (study) every day after school.
Fish ______ (swim) in water.
My brother ______ (play) football on weekends.
The Earth ______ (revolve) around the Sun.
Lesson 4:  Passive Voice
What it means and why it matters

In the active voice, the subject does the action: “The cat chased the mouse.” In the passive voice, the subject receives the action: “The mouse was chased by the cat.” We use the passive when we want to focus on the action, or on who it happened to, rather than who did it.
How to form and use it
Move the object to the front: “The teacher reads the book” → “The book …”
Add the correct form of to be (is, are, was, were): “The book is …”
Add the past participle of the verb: “The book is read …”
Add the doer with by only if you want to: “The book is read by the teacher.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “The meal is cook by the chef.”  ✗
Fix: use the past participle: “The meal is cooked by the chef.”  ✓
Memory tip
Ask: “What happens to the subject?” If the subject receives the action, you are using the passive voice.
Practice 4
Change each active sentence into the passive voice:
The artist paints the picture.
The team won the game.
The students finished their homework.
Lesson 5:  Connectives of Sequence
What it means and why it matters
Connectives of sequence are words that show the order of steps or events — First, Next, Then, After that, Finally. They make instructions and stories easy to follow.
How to form and use it
Begin the step with a connective: First, …  Then, …  Finally, …
Put a comma after the connective when it starts a sentence.
Keep the steps in the correct order so the reader can follow.
A common mistake
Mistake: “First discover the problem.”  ✗  (no comma)
Fix: put a comma after the connective: “First, discover the problem.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of the connectives as a treasure map — each one leads you to the next step. Don't skip a step!
Practice 5
Fill each blank with a sequence connective (First, Next, Finally):
______ , wash your hands.
______ , dry your hands with a towel.
______ , enjoy your snack!
Lesson 6:  Imperatives
What it means and why it matters
Imperatives are commands or instructions that tell someone to do something — “Sit down”, “Erase your mistakes”. They help us communicate clearly and directly.
How to form and use it
Use the base verb with no “to”:  Clean.  Erase.  Listen.
Add details if you like:  “Erase your mistakes neatly!”
To say NOT to do something, start with Don't:  “Don't run in the hall.”
Add please to be polite:  “Please close the door.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “To clean your room.”  ✗
Fix: imperatives use the base verb with no “to”: “Clean your room.”  ✓
Memory tip
An imperative is a friendly “do” command. It usually starts with the action word itself.
Practice 6
Complete each command with the base form of the verb in brackets:
______ (eat) your vegetables!
______ (close) the window, please.
______ (not talk) during the film.
______ (help) your friend with the project.
______ (bring) your book to class tomorrow.
Lesson 7:  Descriptive Starters
What it means and why it matters

A descriptive starter puts a describing word right at the front of your sentence, near the noun. Instead of “The service is good,” you can say “This high-quality service is very helpful.” It makes your writing more interesting and clear.
How to form and use it
Choose a noun: “service”.
Choose a describing word: “high-quality”.
Put them together at the start: “This high-quality service …”
Finish the sentence: “This high-quality service is very helpful.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “This high-quality services are …”  ✗  (singular and plural mixed up)
Fix: match a singular noun with a singular verb: “This high-quality service is …”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of a descriptive starter as “dressing up” your sentence — the describing word is the nice outfit your noun wears at the front.
Practice 7
Add a describing word at the start of each sentence:
______ dog is playful.
______ book is interesting.
______ game is exciting.
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Countable / Uncountable)
1. many   2. some   3. few   4. an   5. many
Practice 2 (There is / There are)
1. There is   2. There are   3. There is   4. There are
Practice 3 (Present Simple)
1. bark   2. studies   3. swim   4. plays   5. revolves
Practice 4 (Passive Voice)
The picture is painted (by the artist).
The game was won (by the team).
The homework was finished (by the students).
Practice 5 (Connectives of Sequence)
1. First   2. Next   3. Finally
Practice 6 (Imperatives)
Eat your vegetables!
Close the window, please.
Don't talk during the film.
Help your friend with the project.
Bring your book to class tomorrow.
Practice 7 (Descriptive Starters)

Answers will vary. Example answers: 1. This playful dog is playful → “This friendly dog is playful.”   2. “This exciting book is interesting.”   3. “This fast game is exciting.”  (Any sensible describing word that matches is correct — ask your AI tutor to check yours.)
