paragraphs: Unit 4: Community and Communication
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 4! This unit is all about community and communication — the many ways people share news, ideas, and help with one another. You will read about a library service, a young reporter, and how a whole town shares information. You will learn strong new words like service, deliver, knowledge, information, discover, and priority, and you will use them to talk and write about your own community. By the end, you will be ready to share a message clearly, just like Ama does in this unit's story, “The Circular Plan”.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Talk about services and communication in your community, using new Unit 4 words.
Describe places and things using There is and There are.
State facts about your town using the present simple (“Mail carriers deliver letters daily”).
Use countable and uncountable nouns correctly (much information, many books).
Give clear instructions using imperatives and connectives of sequence (First… Then… Finally…).
Write a set of instructions, a description, and a short report about your community.
Understand and enjoy the story “The Circular Plan” and talk about its message.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn four or five words each day and do the practice.
Work through the seven mini-lessons in the Grammar file, and check your answers.
Read the Story file, “The Circular Plan”, and answer the questions.
Come back here and work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see everything you have learned.
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Read each passage slowly. When you meet a word from your Vocabulary list, say it out loud. Then answer the questions in your notebook.
Reading 1 — The Library That Came to Us

In the town of Berbera, by the warm sea, there is a small library. It is a plain white building with a blue door, but inside it is full of treasure: shelves of fiction stories and shelves of true books packed with knowledge. The librarian, Mr. Cabdi, wanted every child to read, even the children who lived far away. So he began a new service. Every Thursday, he loads books into a cart and travels to the villages to deliver them. “A library is not the building,” he says. “It is the books, and the people who read them. My priority is to bring information and good stories to everyone.” The children wait by the road. When they discover a new book they love, their faces shine like the sea at sunrise.
After reading, answer:
What new service did Mr. Cabdi begin? How often does he do it?
What does Mr. Cabdi say his priority is?
Find two things you can borrow from the library. Is one fiction and one true?
Why do you think Mr. Cabdi says “a library is not the building”?
Reading 2 — Hodan the Young Reporter

Hodan is nine years old, and she wants to be a scientist one day. But right now, her big challenge is her class newspaper. Every week, she must find real information about her town and share it. Last week, Hodan learned that the population of her neighbourhood was growing, because many new families had arrived. “That is important news,” said her teacher. “Write it clearly, and do not judge the new families — welcome them.” Hodan wrote her report neatly. She was careful to erase every mistake before she stuck the pages down. Her friend Yusuf tried to tease her: “A newspaper by a nine-year-old?” But when he read it, he stopped teasing. “This is high-quality work,” he said. “Can I help next week?”
After reading, answer:
What is Hodan's weekly challenge?
What news did she discover about her neighbourhood?
What did the teacher tell her not to do to the new families?
How did Yusuf's feeling change after he read the newspaper? Why?
Listening — The Town Meeting
Listen to this short talk. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud slowly, then read it yourself.)

“Good evening, everyone, and thank you for coming. Tonight I want to share some information about our new community centre. First, there is a reading room with many books. Then, there is a small clinic where a nurse gives health advice. Finally, there is an open hall for meetings and lessons. This is a service for all of us. Please spread the news, tell your neighbours, and bring your ideas. Communication is how a community grows strong.”
After listening, answer:
How many rooms does the new community centre have? Name each one.
Which two connectives of sequence did the speaker use? (First… Then… Finally…)
What does the speaker ask people to do at the end?
The speaker says, “There is a reading room” but “there are many books.” Why is one is and one are? (See Grammar Lesson 2.)
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — Describe a Service in Your Community
Think of a service in your community: a shop, a mosque, a clinic, a market, or a library. Get ready to say three or four sentences about it. Use these sentence starters:
“In my community, there is a ______.”
“The location of it is ______.”
“People go there to ______.”
“I think this service is ______ (helpful / important / friendly).”
Record yourself. Check: Did you use there is correctly? Did you speak in clear, full sentences?
Speaking 2 — Give Clear Instructions
Teach someone how to do a simple task — how to make tea, how to wash your hands, or how to send a message. Use imperatives and sequence words:
“After that, ______.”
“Finally, ______.”
Speaking Game — Twenty Questions: Guess the Job
Play with your AI tutor or your family. One person thinks of a community helper (a scientist, a teacher, a nurse, a mail carrier, a shopkeeper). The others ask yes/no questions to guess it:
Ask: “Do you deliver things?”  “Do you work with knowledge and books?”
The thinker answers only “Yes, I do” or “No, I don't.”
Guess the job in twenty questions or fewer. Then swap turns.
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — Instructions: “How to Share News in My Community”
Write a set of instructions with at least four steps. Use a sequence word to begin each step, and use imperatives (action words). For example:
First, find real information. Then, write it clearly. After that, check your spelling. Finally, share it with your neighbours.
Writing 2 — Description: “A Circular Object in Nature”
Write four or five sentences describing something circular you have seen — the sun, the moon, a drum, a plate, a wheel. Begin with a descriptive starter: “This bright, circular moon is…”
Writing 3 — A Short Report: “My Town”
Write three or four sentences of true information about your town. You could write about its population, its location, or a service people use. Use the present simple for facts.
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Do each activity, then check your answers in the key below.
Activity 1 — Match the Word to its Meaning
Match each word (1–5) to its meaning (a–e).
service
deliver
knowledge
population
priority
a) the number of people living in a place
b) something that is more important than other things
c) help or work done for others
d) to bring something to a person or place
e) what you know or learn
Activity 2 — There is or There are?
Choose the correct phrase.
______ a library near my house.
______ many books on the shelf.
______ some information on the board.
______ three scientists in the lab.
Activity 3 — much or many?
Choose much (for uncountable nouns) or many (for countable nouns).
How ______ books are in the library?
How ______ information did you find?
There are too ______ people in the hall.
We do not have ______ time left.
Activity 4 — Put the Steps in Order
Add the sequence words First, Then, After that, Finally to these steps for sharing a message:
______ , decide what you want to say.
______ , write it down clearly.
______ , check your spelling.
______ , give the message to the right person.
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand countable and uncountable nouns. Can you explain it another way?”
Practise speaking: “Can you listen to my instructions and tell me if they are clear?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my report about my town and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me five more ‘there is / there are’ questions?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can talk about a service in my community.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use there is and there are correctly.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use much and many correctly.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can give clear instructions with sequence words.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can write a short report of facts about my town.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can retell the story “The Circular Plan” and its message.   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 5
Our next unit is Unit 5: Action and Movement. You will learn lots of action words and how to describe how and when things happen.
Make sure you know all the Unit 4 words on your Word Wall.
Notice action words all around you this week: run, jump, carry, build, deliver.
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 5? Can you get me ready?”
Well done — you are becoming a strong communicator! A good learner keeps practising a little every day. Keep it up! 🌟

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 4: Community and Communication
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Circular Plan”
The Circular Plan

Ama lived in a small town near the coast of Ghana. Her school was plain on the outside, but it was full of energy inside. Every week, the teacher gave the class a new challenge — something to learn or build together as a team.
One morning, the teacher wrote on the board: “Project: Create a way to share information with the community.”
Ama raised her hand. “Like a newspaper?”
“Something like that,” the teacher said. “But you must use what you already know. Use your knowledge, and make something useful.”
The students talked during break. “We could build a big sign!” said Kojo.
“No,” said Yaa. “People will just walk past it. Let's make a box with papers inside.”
“That's still too plain,” Ama added. “It has to be circular. That's my rule!”
“Why circular?” Kojo teased. “Is your brain a wheel?” Ama laughed, but she did not answer. She already had an idea.

After school, Ama went to the local library. She looked at the location of every fiction shelf, every news board, and every reading chair. She found a dusty corner near the door and smiled. “There is space here,” she whispered.
The librarian came over. “Can I help you?”
“We want to deliver news and stories to the whole town,” Ama replied.
“That's a big job,” the librarian said kindly. “But good service always starts small.”
The next day, Ama and her friends arrived early. They brought cardboard, tape, scissors, and markers.
“What are you building?” asked a younger boy.
“A circular news station!” Ama answered. “People will stop here and spin the circle. Each part gives different information — the weather, stories, or health tips.”
Kojo frowned. “That sounds hard.”
“It's a challenge,” Ama agreed. “But we can do it.”
The teacher came by and nodded. “Very creative,” she said. “Now check your spelling before you stick anything down. And don't forget to erase the pencil lines.”
The group worked neatly. Ama drew the circle. Kojo wrote the headlines. Yaa added a funny comic about a scientist and a dancing goat, and everyone laughed.
“We need facts too,” Ama said, “not just fiction.”
“There is a doctor in town,” said Kojo. “Let's write about health.”
“There are new shops opening as well,” added Yaa.
“Write clearly. That is our top priority,” Ama reminded them.
By Friday, the project was ready. They placed it near the school gate. It stood tall and colourful.
The next morning, a group of parents and children gathered around it. “What is this?” asked a man.
“It's called ‘The Circular Plan',” Ama explained. “Turn it, and read what's on each side.”
A woman read aloud, “Tips for clean water.” A boy spun it and found the comic. “A goat that becomes a scientist?” he laughed.
“It's just fiction!” Kojo shouted, grinning. People laughed and clapped. “This is good service,” someone said. “Our children are teaching us.”
One parent pointed. “But there is no news about the market. That's what people care about.”
“Then please help us write it!” Ama replied. “We want real thought from the people too.”
Another woman smiled. “That's a good priority — sharing everyone's voice.”
The teacher stood beside the project. “This,” she said, “is how we build a community. We learn, we teach, and we grow. Today, our students became leaders.”
As the crowd left, a boy began to tease Kojo about his handwriting. “Your words look like little bugs!” he laughed. Kojo grinned. “I wrote them in a hurry. I'll fix them later.”
“Good idea,” said Ama. “We must always check for quality.”
That afternoon, Ama looked at the circle again. “It may look plain,” she said to Yaa, “but the work inside it matters.”
Yaa nodded. “It's not about how big the quantity is,” she said. “It's about what people learn.”
And from that day on, people in Ama's town stopped by the spinning circle, learned something new, and sometimes even added their own page.
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
What project did the teacher give the class?
Why did Ama want the news station to be circular?
Where did Ama decide to put ‘The Circular Plan'? Why that location?
What was the group's top priority when writing?
One parent said something was missing. What was it, and how did Ama answer?
What did Yaa mean when she said, “It's not about how big the quantity is”?
Think and talk
The teacher said, “Good service always starts small.” What do you think she meant?
How does ‘The Circular Plan' help the community communicate? Could something like it help your community?
Kojo teased Ama, and later a boy teased Kojo. When is teasing friendly, and when can it hurt someone?
The teacher said, “This is how we build a community.” What does your community do to share news and help one another?
Find the words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 4 words and say the sentence they are in:
service   ·   deliver   ·   information   ·   knowledge   ·   challenge   ·   priority
location   ·   circular   ·   plain   ·   fiction   ·   scientist   ·   thought   ·   quantity   ·   quality   ·   erase   ·   tease
Now You Try:  Writing
Imagine your class will make its own way to share news. Write four sentences describing your idea. Use the words information, service, and community.
Write a short comic or story (like Yaa's dancing goat) that a scientist could read. Draw a picture to go with it.
Write three true facts about your own town for a news page. Use the present simple (for example: “Our town has a busy market.”).

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 5: Action and Movement
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Four grammar lessons

This unit has four small grammar lessons. For each one: read what it means, look at how to form it, study the mistake to avoid (✗ → ✓), remember the tip, and then try the practice. Check your answers in the Answer Key at the end. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1:  Past Simple Tense
What it means and why it matters

The past simple tense is used to talk about actions that already happened and are finished. It lets us tell stories about the past — what we did yesterday or last week. For example: “She rescued the bird yesterday.”  “They galloped across the field.”
How to form it
Regular verbs: add -ed to the base verb.  play → played, check → checked, gallop → galloped.
Irregular verbs: these change in their own way.  go → went, run → ran, find → found, eat → ate.
The pattern is: Subject + past verb + (when).  “We found a cave last Saturday.”
To make it negative, use did not + base verb:  “She did not rescue the bird.”
To ask a question, use Did + subject + base verb:  “Did she rescue the bird?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “Yesterday she rescue the bird.”  ✗   (base verb, no ending)
Fix: Add -ed for the past: “Yesterday she rescued the bird.”  ✓
Also watch irregulars — “I goed” ✗ becomes “I went” ✓.
Memory tip
Say to yourself: “Yesterday is history!” If it already happened, the verb changes — usually by adding -ed.
Practice 1
Write the correct past simple form of the verb in brackets.
They ______ (play) in the park last Sunday.
She ______ (find) a lost dog on the way home.
We ______ (check) the tunnel before we went in.
He ______ (go) to the market after school.
The horse ______ (gallop) across the field.
Lesson 2:  Adjective + Noun Phrases
What it means and why it matters

An adjective is a word that describes something. A noun is a word that names a person, place, or thing. When we put an adjective before a noun, we make a clearer, more interesting phrase. For example: “a greedy fox”, “a spiral staircase”.
How to form it
Choose an adjective (a describing word): greedy, spiral, hungry, shiny, tall.
Choose a noun (a naming word): fox, staircase, dog, car, tree.
Put the adjective before the noun:  a greedy fox,  a spiral staircase,  a shiny car.
If you use two adjectives, keep it simple: “a big blue ball”, not a long list.
A common mistake
Mistake: “a fox greedy”.  ✗   (adjective after the noun)
Fix: In English the adjective goes first: “a greedy fox”.  ✓
Memory tip
Adjectives are helpers that go in front. Think: “Describe it first, then name it — greedy fox!”
Practice 2
Add an adjective to complete each phrase. (One good answer is given as a clue.)
a ______ staircase   (clue: goes round and round)
a ______ dog   (clue: it has not eaten)
a ______ race   (clue: it makes you cheer)
a ______ fox   (clue: it wants everything)
Lesson 3:  Adverbs of Manner
What it means and why it matters

An adverb of manner tells us how an action happens. Since this unit is all about action and movement, these words make your sentences come alive. For example: “The horse galloped quickly.”  “We walked carefully into the cave.”
How to form it
Most adverbs of manner are made by adding -ly to an adjective.  slow → slowly, quick → quickly, quiet → quietly.
If the adjective ends in -y, change y to i and add -ly.  happy → happily, easy → easily.
The adverb usually comes after the verb:  “She ran quickly.”  “He spoke softly.”
A few are irregular: good → well.  “He plays football well.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “The snail moved slow.”  ✗   (that is an adjective)
Fix: Use the -ly adverb to say how: “The snail moved slowly.”  ✓
Memory tip
If the word answers “How?”, it usually ends in -ly. How did she run? Quickly!
Practice 3
Change the adjective in brackets into an adverb of manner to finish the sentence.
The children walked ______ (careful) into the dark tunnel.
The car moved ______ (quick) down the road.
She opened the gift ______ (quiet) so it would stay a surprise.
The old man spoke ______ (slow) and kindly.
Lesson 4:  Imperatives and Modal Verbs (can / must)
What it means and why it matters

An imperative gives an instruction or a command — perfect for how-to guides. A modal verb like can or must tells us what is possible or what is important. For example: “Check your work!”  “You must be careful.”  “Birds can whistle tunes.”
How to form it
Imperative: start with the base verb, with no subject.  “Proceed carefully.”  “Peek inside.”
To say “do not”, add don't:  “Don't run near the cave.”
can = able to / possible:  “A horse can gallop.”  Keep the base verb after it: can gallop, not can gallops.
must = it is important / a rule:  “You must check that it is safe.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “You must to check the cave.”  ✗   Also: “A bird can whistles.”  ✗
Fix: No “to”, and keep the base verb: “You must check the cave.”  ✓   “A bird can whistle.”  ✓
Memory tip
After can and must, the verb stays in its plain base form — “can go”, “must stop”. And an imperative jumps straight to the action: “No ‘you’, just the verb!”
Practice 4
Choose the correct word or form in brackets.
______ (Check / Checks) your answers before you finish.  (make it a command)
A horse can ______ (gallop / gallops) very fast.
You must ______ (proceed / to proceed) carefully in the dark.
______ (Don't / Not) touch the wet paint!
Answer Key
Practice 1 — Past Simple Tense
1. played   2. found   3. checked   4. went   5. galloped
Practice 2 — Adjective + Noun Phrases
Example answers: 1. a spiral staircase   2. a hungry dog   3. an exciting race   4. a greedy fox
Practice 3 — Adverbs of Manner
1. carefully   2. quickly   3. quietly   4. slowly
Practice 4 — Imperatives and Modal Verbs
1. Check   2. gallop   3. proceed   4. Don't

paragraphs: Unit 5: Action and Movement
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 5! This unit is all about action and movement — how people and animals move, how we describe fast and slow actions, and how we make good choices when we feel excited or under pressure. You will read about a runner who learns to accelerate, a horse that gallops across a field, and children who rescue a lost animal. You will meet strong verbs like squeeze, peek, proceed, and defend, and you will learn to say exactly *how* an action happens — quickly, slowly, or carefully. By the end, you will be ready to enjoy this unit's adventure story, “The Spiral Cave”.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use strong action verbs to describe how people and animals move.
Tell a story about the past using the past simple tense (played, galloped, rescued, went).
Describe actions with adverbs of manner (quickly, slowly, carefully).
Build clear adjective + noun phrases (a greedy fox, a spiral staircase).
Give instructions with imperatives and use can and must correctly.
Read and understand an adventure story and talk about the choices the characters make.
Write a short how-to guide and a narrative about an exciting event.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few action words each day and use them in sentences.
Work through the four mini-lessons in the Grammar file (past simple, adjective + noun, adverbs of manner, imperatives & modals).
Read the Story file, “The Spiral Cave”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.
Part 1:  Reading and Listening
Reading 1 — The Race at the Village Field
Read this text carefully:

On Saturday morning, the children met at the village field for the running race. Amina felt a lot of pressure, because everyone was watching. When the teacher blew his whistle, the runners began. At first Amina ran slowly. Then she started to accelerate. Her heart rate grew faster and faster. In the next field, a horse began to gallop beside the fence, as if it wanted to race too! Amina did not look back. She ran quickly but carefully, and she crossed the line first. “Well done,” said her teacher. “You stayed calm under pressure.”
After reading, answer:
What sound told the runners to begin?
Find two words in the text that mean “to go faster” or “how fast something is”.
Find the two adverbs (words ending in -ly) that describe how Amina ran.
Why did the teacher say Amina did well?
Reading 2 — How Animals Move
Read this short information text:

Animals move in many different ways. A horse can gallop across the grass. A snail moves slowly in a spiral shape. A cat will squeeze through a narrow gap. When a bird sees danger, it may give a signal to warn the others, and the whole group will fly away to prevent being caught. Some animals suffer when there is no water, so they must move far to find a river. Watching how animals move can excite us and teach us about the world.
After reading, answer:
Name three different ways animals move in this text.
Why might a bird give a signal to the others?
What happens to some animals when there is no water?
Which way of moving do you find most interesting? Why?
Listening — The Lost Goat (script)
Ask your AI tutor to read this aloud slowly, then read it yourself.

“Yesterday, Yusuf lost his little goat near the market. He began to gaze in every direction. ‘I must find her before dark,’ he said. He decided to check behind the shops. First he took a peek into the alley. Then he proceeded slowly. Suddenly he heard a soft sound. There was the goat! Yusuf ran to rescue her. ‘I will always defend you,’ he said, and he carried her home.”
After listening, answer:
What did Yusuf lose, and where?
What did he do first, and what did he do next? (Listen for the sequencing words.)
Find the verb that means “to save someone from danger”.
This story happened in the past. Name three past-tense verbs you can hear.
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — Describe an Action
Choose an action and describe it using an adverb of manner (a word that says *how*). Practise saying:
“The horse galloped quickly across the field.”
“The snail moved slowly up the wall.”
“I opened the door carefully so it would not make a noise.”
Now make three sentences of your own about actions you did today. Record yourself and listen back.
Speaking 2 — Tell About a Time You Felt Pressure
Talk for one minute about a time you felt pressure or excitement — maybe a race, a test, or reciting the Qur'an in front of others. Try these sentence starters:
“One day, I had to ______.”
“I felt ______ because ______.”
“First I ______. Then I ______.”
Speaking Game — Act and Guess
Play with your AI tutor, a friend, or your family:
One person acts out an action word from the Vocabulary list (squeeze, gallop, peek, whistle, gaze).
The other person guesses: “Are you ______ing?”
Answer: “Yes, I am!” or “No, try again.” Then swap turns.
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — How-To Guide: How to Rescue a Lost Animal
Write a short how-to guide with 4–5 steps. Use imperatives (Check…, Move…, Proceed…) and sequencing words (First, Then, After that, Finally). For example:
First, move slowly so you do not frighten the animal. Then, check that it is not hurt…
Writing 2 — Narrative: An Exciting Adventure

Write a short story (6–10 sentences) about an exciting adventure where someone moves quickly and makes a good choice. Use the past simple tense all the way through. Try to include at least five words from your Vocabulary list, and underline them.
Part 4:  Activities and Exercises
Activity 1 — Match the Action
Match each verb to its meaning. Write the pairs (for example: 1 - c).
gallop
peek
rescue
accelerate
a. to look quickly or secretly
b. to save someone from danger
c. to run fast like a horse
d. to go faster
Activity 2 — Past or Now?
Write P for past or N for now (present) for each sentence.
The horse gallops every morning.
Yesterday she rescued a bird.
They checked the cave carefully.
I squeeze the orange each day.
Activity 3 — Add the Adverb
Add an adverb of manner (quickly, slowly, carefully, quietly) to finish each sentence.
The children walked ______ into the dark tunnel.
The car moved ______ down the empty road.
She opened the gift ______ so it would stay a surprise.
Activity 4 — Build a Phrase
Put an adjective before each noun to make a phrase, like “a greedy fox”.
a ______ staircase
a ______ dog
a ______ race
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand the past tense. Can you explain it another way?”
Practise speaking: “How do I say this word? Can you listen and help me?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my story and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more sentences to change into the past tense?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can use strong action verbs to describe movement.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can tell a story in the past simple tense.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use adverbs of manner (quickly, slowly, carefully).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can make adjective + noun phrases.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can give instructions with imperatives and use can and must.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can understand the story and talk about the characters' choices.   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 6:  People in Society
Our next unit is Unit 6: People in Society. Get ready:
Make sure you know all the Unit 5 action words on your Word Wall.
In Unit 6 you will talk about the people around us — the jobs they do and how we help one another. Start noticing the helpers in your town: the shopkeeper, the imam, the doctor, the teacher.
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 6? Can you get me ready for People in Society?”
Well done — you are becoming a strong reader and storyteller. Keep practising every day, and be proud of every new word you learn! 🌟

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 5: Action and Movement
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Spiral Cave”
The Spiral Cave

One afternoon, in a quiet village near the forest, three children — Mado, Timo, and Fara — were playing beside the river. They often made up games and told stories, but today they found something strange. Behind a pile of rocks, there was a dark hole in the ground.
“Let me peek inside!” said Timo.
Fara held his hand. “Be careful. It might not be safe.”
But Mado had already taken a step forward. “It is dark, but I can see something. The floor goes down in a spiral.”
“Like a staircase?” asked Timo.
“Yes! Let us explore it!” said Mado, feeling excited.
The children looked around for a strong stick and a torch. “We must check that it is safe,” said Fara. “We do not want to fall.”
They went in slowly. “First, peek inside. Then, proceed carefully,” whispered Mado, copying what their teacher always said in science class.
Inside, the air was cold. The spiral tunnel turned and turned. Timo pointed to the wall. “Look! A drawing of people… and a lion?”
They kept walking until they reached a wider space. “It is a cave!” shouted Fara. Their voices echoed off the stone.
Suddenly, they heard a soft sound.
“Was that a whistle?” asked Mado.
“No,” said Timo. “That was an animal!”
Then, out of the shadows, two eyes appeared. A small dog — thin and shivering — stepped forward. It wagged its tail.
“It looks hungry,” said Fara. “We have to rescue it!”
Mado reached into his bag and pulled out a piece of bread. He called gently to the dog. “Come here, boy.”
The dog sniffed and walked closer. “I think he is friendly,” said Timo. “He does not want to hurt us.”
“We should give him a name,” said Mado.
“Not yet,” said Fara. “First we need to check that he is not sick.”
The children carried the dog out of the cave. Outside, the sun was low in the sky.
“We must accelerate,” said Timo. “It is getting late!”
Back in the village, they went to the animal helper, Mama Luma. She was kind and wise.
“Where did you find him?” she asked.
“In a spiral tunnel near the river,” said Mado.
“You were lucky,” said Mama Luma. “That tunnel is very old. There is pressure from the soil above it. It could fall in at any time.”
“We did not know,” said Fara. “We were too excited.”
“Next time you must tell your parents first,” she said. “But I am glad you brought him to me.”
The dog was given clean water and food. He wagged his tail and let Fara squeeze his paw gently.
“He is strong,” said Mama Luma. “He only needs rest.”
“But who left him there?” asked Timo.
“We may never know,” said Mama Luma. “Sometimes people are greedy. They want an animal but do not care for it. That is not right. An animal like this can suffer when no one looks after it.”
The children were quiet. They did not like the thought of anyone being so unkind.
“We can help him,” said Fara. “Let us make posters and describe him. Someone might be looking for him.”
“That is a kind plan,” said Mama Luma.
That night, Mado wrote in his notebook:
“We found a cave, a spiral path, and a small dog. We learned to move slowly and to think. We felt pressure and fear, but we stayed strong.”
The next day, the children shared their story at school. Their teacher smiled. “You followed instructions, stayed calm, and made good choices. That is something to be proud of.”
“What should we name him now?” asked Timo.
“How about Simba?” said Fara. “He is strong, like a lion.”
“Perfect,” said Mado. “He is our little rescued lion.”
Simba barked — happy, and safe at last.
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
What did the three children find behind the pile of rocks?
What shape did the floor of the tunnel make as it went down?
What animal did the children find inside the cave? How did it look?
Why did Mama Luma say the children were lucky?
What kind plan did Fara suggest to help find the dog's owner?
What name did the children choose for the dog, and why?
Think and talk
The children were excited, but they still tried to be careful. Why is it important to think before you act?
Mama Luma said people should not be greedy with animals. What does it mean to care for an animal properly?
Have you ever helped an animal or a person in trouble? What did you do?
The teacher said the children “made good choices”. Which choice do you think was the best one? Why?
Find the words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 5 words and say the sentence they are in:
peek
spiral
proceed
whistle
rescue
accelerate
pressure
squeeze
greedy
describe
Now You Try:  Writing
Imagine you found the cave with the children. Write four sentences about what you would do. Use the past simple tense (I walked…, I checked…) and at least two words from the Vocabulary list.
Write a short how-to guide called “How to Rescue a Lost Animal”. Use imperatives (Move…, Check…, Proceed…) and the words First, Then, and Finally.
Draw Mado, Timo, Fara, and Simba. Write one sentence under your picture describing what is happening.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 6: People in Society
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Eight grammar mini-lessons

This unit has eight small grammar lessons. They are the tools you need to talk about the people in your town and the jobs they do. Read one lesson at a time. For each one, learn what it means, see how to build it, spot the common mistake, remember the tip, and then try the practice. Check your answers in the Answer Key at the end. Say every example out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1:  Present Simple
What it means and why it matters

We use the Present Simple to talk about routines, facts, and things that happen again and again. It is perfect for describing jobs, because most jobs are the same every day: “The janitor cleans the school every morning.”
How to build it
I / you / we / they + base verb:  “They build roads.”
he / she / it + verb + s / es:  “The engineer designs bridges.”
Negatives use don't / doesn't:  “She doesn't work on Fridays.”
Questions use Do / Does:  “Does a lawyer help people?”
Add es for verbs ending in -sh, -ch, -x, or -o: “watches”, “goes”, “fixes”. If a verb ends in a consonant + y, change y to i and add es: “carry” → “carries”.
A common mistake
Mistake: “She play soccer.”  ✗
Fix: For he / she / it, don't forget the s: “She plays soccer.”  ✓
Mistake: “Does she plays soccer?”  ✗   Fix: after does, use the base verb: “Does she play soccer?”  ✓
Memory tip
“He, she, it — add an s to it!” Think of the s as a little superhero cape that only he, she, and it get to wear.
Practice 1
Write the correct Present Simple form of the verb in brackets.
The cat (sleep) ______ on the sofa every afternoon.
My mother (cook) ______ dinner in the evening.
They (play) ______ football every weekend.
The teacher (give) ______ homework on Fridays.
She (not like) ______ broccoli.
Lesson 2:  Imperatives
What it means and why it matters

Imperatives are sentences that give a command, an instruction, or a piece of advice. They tell someone what to do — or what not to do. We use them for rules, recipes, directions, and kind advice, like “Be kind to new immigrants.”
How to build it
Start with the verb. You do not need the word “you”:  “Listen carefully.”
For a negative command, put don't before the verb:  “Don't forget your lunch.”
Use please to be polite:  “Please close the door.”
Use let's to suggest doing something together:  “Let's help the new pupil.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “You listen to the teacher.”  ✗
Fix: An imperative does not need a subject. Just start with the verb: “Listen to the teacher.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of a coach shouting to the team: “Run! Pass! Shoot!” A coach never says “You run” — just the action word. Imperatives are friendly, quick reminders.
Practice 2
Complete each command with the correct imperative verb from the brackets.
______ your hands before eating. (wash)
______ your voice when the teacher is talking. (lower)
______ to your parents. (listen)
______ your toys after playing. (put away)
______ to be on time. (try)
Lesson 3:  Modal Verbs — should and mustn't
What it means and why it matters

Modal verbs help us talk about advice and rules. Should gives friendly advice — a good idea. Mustn't is a strong rule — something you are not allowed to do. Both help us live well together in a community.
How to build it
should + base verb (advice):  “You should thank the helpers.”
mustn't + base verb (a rule, not allowed):  “You mustn't ignore a tenant's needs.”
should can ask a question:  “Should I help you?”
The opposite of should is shouldn't:  “You shouldn't be rude.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “You shouldn't to go.”  ✗
Fix: After a modal verb, use the base verb only — no “to”: “You shouldn't go.”  ✓   Also remember: must = you have to; mustn't = you must not.
Memory tip
Should is a friendly guide saying, “Hey, this is a good idea!” Mustn't is a strict red sign saying, “Stop! You can't do this!”
Practice 3
Complete each sentence with should or mustn't.
You ______ forget to brush your teeth before bed.
We ______ help our friends when they need it.
You ______ play video games all night.
Students ______ listen to the teacher in class.
Lesson 4:  Prepositions of Direction and Place
What it means and why it matters

Prepositions are small words that tell us where something is or which way it is going. They make our sentences clear: “The refugee walked towards the building” tells us the direction, while “The book is on the shelf” tells us the place.
How to build it
Direction (where something is going): towards, across, by.
Place (where something is): on, in, near.
Direction example:  “She walked across the street.”
Place example:  “The school is near the library.”  /  “The toys are in the box.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “The apples are on the basket.”  ✗
Fix: The apples are inside, so use in: “The apples are in the basket.”  ✓
Memory tip
Draw a little map in your mind: in = inside a box, on = resting on a table, near = close beside, towards = walking to a sign, across = over a bridge, by = standing side by side.
Practice 4
Use a word from the box to complete each sentence:  across  ·  towards  ·  on  ·  in  ·  near  ·  by
The bird flew ______ the tree.
The dog is sitting ______ the grass.
She walked ______ the street to get to the store.
The pencil is ______ the table.
The library is ______ my school.
He ran ______ the finish line.
Lesson 5:  Past Simple
What it means and why it matters

The Past Simple tells us about actions that already finished. We use it for stories and for the lives of real people: “The lawyer helped many families.” It lets us share what happened yesterday, last week, or long ago.
How to build it
Regular verbs: add -ed:  “play” → “played”, “start” → “started”.
Verbs ending in -e: just add -d:  “like” → “liked”.
Irregular verbs change completely:  “go” → “went”, “see” → “saw”, “have” → “had”.
The verb stays the same for every subject:  “I played”, “she played”, “they played”.
A common mistake
Mistake: “He help many people.”  ✗
Fix: The action is finished, so change the verb: “He helped many people.”  ✓   And don't forget the subject: not “Gave a speech” but “She gave a speech.”
Memory tip
The Past Simple is a time machine. When you use it, you travel back to something that already happened. Remember: Regular = -ed, Irregular = a special form you learn by heart.
Practice 5
Write the Past Simple form of the verb in brackets.
Last summer, I ______ (visit) my grandparents.
The teacher ______ (give) us a test last week.
They ______ (play) video games all evening.
She ______ (make) a delicious cake for the party.
He ______ (write) a letter to his friend.
Lesson 6:  Past Continuous
What it means and why it matters

The Past Continuous describes an action that was happening at a certain moment in the past. It is great for stories, especially when one action is interrupted by another: “The merchant was working when the rain started.”
How to build it
was / were + verb + -ing.
Use was with I, he, she, it:  “I was reading.”
Use were with you, we, they:  “They were playing.”
Often used with when or while:  “She was studying when her friend called.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “They were play in the yard.”  ✗
Fix: Don't forget the -ing: “They were playing in the yard.”  ✓   Also, state verbs like *know* and *love* do not use -ing: not “I was knowing” but “I knew”.
Memory tip
Think of the Past Continuous as a movie of the past. Say “I was watching…” and picture the action still unfolding, until something interrupts it.
Practice 6
Write the Past Continuous form of the verb(s) in brackets.
The cat ______ (sleep) on the couch when the doorbell rang.
We ______ (play) outside when it started to rain.
She ______ (read) a book while he ______ (watch) TV.
Lesson 7:  Descriptive Verbs and Similes
What it means and why it matters

A descriptive verb paints a clearer picture of an action, and a simile compares two things using like or as. Together they make your writing exciting: “The janitor worked like a machine” shows us he was fast and never stopped.
How to build it
Choose a strong verb instead of a plain one:  “run” → “dash” or “sprint”.
Think of something similar to compare it to.
Join them with like or as:  “He dashed like a cheetah.”
Put it in a full sentence:  “The dog barked like a lion.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “He is like a hero.”  ✗ (this describes who he is, not what he does)
Fix: Use like to compare an action: “He fights like a hero.”  ✓   Also, swap weak verbs for strong ones: not “ran fast” but “sprinted like the wind.”
Memory tip
A simile is a way to paint a picture with words. Ask yourself: “What is this action like?” Then use like or as to make the picture clear.
Practice 7
Finish each sentence with your own descriptive verb and simile. (Example answers are in the key.)
The cat jumped ______ a ______ .
She wrote her story ______ a ______ .
The thunder rumbled ______ a ______ .
Lesson 8:  Question Words
What it means and why it matters
Question words help us ask for information. Good learners ask lots of questions! We use them in interviews and conversations: “What does a lawyer do?”, “Where does an engineer work?”
How to build it
Who = people  ·  What = things or ideas  ·  Where = places
When = time  ·  Why = reasons  ·  How = the way something is done
Structure: Question word + helping verb + subject + main verb:  “What do you like to eat?”
Who can also be the subject:  “Who is coming?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “What you like?”  ✗
Fix: Don't forget the helping verb do / does: “What do you like?”  ✓
Memory tip
Question words are little detectives. Each one hunts for a different clue: Who (people), What (things), Where (places), When (time), Why (reasons), How (methods).
Practice 8
Fill each blank with the correct question word.
______ is your best friend?
______ do you go to the park?
______ do you want for your birthday?
______ does your family live?
______ do you like ice cream?
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Present Simple)
1. sleeps   2. cooks   3. play   4. gives   5. does not like (doesn't like)
Practice 2 (Imperatives)
1. Wash   2. Lower   3. Listen   4. Put away   5. Try
Practice 3 (should / mustn't)
1. mustn't   2. should   3. mustn't   4. should
Practice 4 (Prepositions)
1. towards   2. in   3. across   4. on   5. near   6. by
Practice 5 (Past Simple)
1. visited   2. gave   3. played   4. made   5. wrote
Practice 6 (Past Continuous)
1. was sleeping   2. were playing   3. was reading / was watching
Practice 7 (Descriptive Verbs and Similes) — example answers
1. The cat jumped like a kangaroo.   2. She wrote her story like a pro.   3. The thunder rumbled like a train.  (Your own answers can be different — ask your AI tutor to check them.)
Practice 8 (Question Words)
1. Who   2. When   3. What   4. Where   5. Why

paragraphs: Unit 6: People in Society
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 6! This unit is all about the people in our society — the many workers, leaders, and helpers who make a community work. You will meet engineers and carpenters, governors and senators, artists and photographers, and you will learn about refugees, immigrants, and the roles we all play. Along the way you will use the Present Simple to describe jobs, should and mustn't to give advice and rules, and question words to interview people about their work. By the end, you will be able to describe the helpers in your own town — just like Fatima does in this unit's story, “The Community Parade”.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Name and describe at least twenty jobs and roles that people play in society.
Explain what different workers and leaders do, using the Present Simple.
Give advice and state rules using should and mustn't.
Ask and answer interview questions using who, what, where, why, and how.
Use prepositions like across, towards, near, and by to say where people are and where they go.
Tell a short story about the past using the Past Simple and Past Continuous.
Write a role reflection, a short newspaper article, and an advice poster.
Explain the difference between a refugee and an immigrant, and say why we welcome both.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn one group of words each day (there are four groups).
Work through the eight short lessons in the Grammar file, one at a time.
Read the Story, “The Community Parade”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills in this lesson: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Reading 1 — The People of Our Town
Read this text about a busy town, then answer the questions.

Every town is full of people who play important roles. Early in the morning, the janitor cleans the school before the pupils arrive. In the market, a merchant sells fresh fruit while a carpenter mends a wooden stall. Near the river, an engineer checks the new bridge, and a laborer carries heavy stones. At the town hall, the governor reads a new plan, and a lawyer helps a family understand the law. We do not always notice these people, but our town could not work without them. Every one of them helps us to live, to learn, and to stay safe.
After reading, answer:
Name four jobs from the text and say what each person does.
Where does the engineer work? Which preposition does the text use to tell you?
The text says our town “could not work without them.” What do you think this means?
Which of these workers have you seen in your own town this week?
Reading 2 — Two Neighbours: A Refugee and an Immigrant
Read this short text.

Amina and her family are refugees. They left their home because it was not safe, and they travelled a long way to find peace. Next door lives Omar, an immigrant. He moved to the town to start a new business and build a better life. Amina's mother says, “A refugee runs from danger. An immigrant walks towards a dream. We should welcome both, because everyone deserves a safe home and a kind neighbour.”
After reading, answer:
Why did Amina's family leave their home?
Why did Omar move to the town?
In your own words, what is the difference between a refugee and an immigrant?
How could you make a new neighbour feel welcome?
Listening — An Interview with a Community Helper
Ask your AI tutor to read this interview aloud, or read it with a partner. One person is the reporter; the other is Mr Diriye, an engineer.
Reporter: “Good morning, Mr Diriye. What does an engineer do?”
Mr Diriye: “Good morning! An engineer designs and builds things, like roads, bridges, and machines. I am building a new bridge for our town.”
Reporter: “Where do you work?”
Mr Diriye: “I work near the river, and sometimes in an office. Today I am standing by the water, checking the plans.”
Reporter: “Why is your work important?”
Mr Diriye: “Because a strong bridge helps people cross safely. You mustn't build in a hurry — you should plan carefully and check everything twice.”
After listening, answer:
What does an engineer do? Give two examples from the interview.
Where is Mr Diriye working today?
What advice does Mr Diriye give, using should and mustn't?
Which question words does the reporter use? (Find three.)
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — Describe a Job
Choose one job from your Vocabulary list. Get ready to say three sentences about it, using the Present Simple:
“A ______ is a person who ______.”
“A ______ helps our community by ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you add the s for “he/she” (“A lawyer helps”)? Did you speak clearly?
Speaking 2 — The Interview Game
Play with your AI tutor or a partner. One person chooses a job in secret. The other asks questions to guess it:
“What do you do?”  →  “I build things from wood.”
“Where do you work?”  →  “I work in a workshop.”
“What do you use?”  →  “I use a hammer and saw.”
Guess the job! (A carpenter.) Then swap turns.
Speaking 3 — Giving Advice
Practise giving kind advice about how to treat people in society, using should and mustn't:
“You should thank the people who help you.”
“You should welcome a new immigrant to your school.”
“You mustn't ignore someone who needs help.”
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — A Role Reflection Paragraph

Choose one job (for example, a janitor, a lawyer, or an engineer). Write four or five sentences about it in the Present Simple. Say who the person is, what they do, where they work, and why their job matters.
Writing 2 — A Short Newspaper Article

Write a short article (about six sentences) about a local hero or community helper — for example, a refugee who started a business, or a carpenter who helped rebuild a school. Give your article a headline in capital letters, like the messenger in the story: “LOCAL HERO HELPS THE TOWN.”
Writing 3 — An Advice Poster

Make a welcome or safety poster for new immigrants or tenants. Write three pieces of advice using should, and one rule using mustn't. For example: “You should say hello to your neighbours.” / “You mustn't be afraid to ask for help.” Then draw a picture to go with it.
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Activity 1 — Who Am I? (Match the job)
Read each clue and write the job.
I clean and take care of buildings. Who am I?
I take pictures with a camera. Who am I?
I help people understand the law. Who am I?
I build things from wood. Who am I?
I carry messages from one person to another. Who am I?
Answers: 1. a janitor  2. a photographer  3. a lawyer  4. a carpenter  5. a messenger
Activity 2 — Present Simple: Add the ‘s’
Write each sentence with the correct Present Simple verb.
The engineer ______ (design) bridges.
The children ______ (thank) the helpers.
A merchant ______ (sell) fruit at the market.
The soldiers ______ (protect) the country.
Answers: 1. designs  2. thank  3. sells  4. protect
Activity 3 — should or mustn't?
Complete each piece of advice.
You ______ welcome a new immigrant to your class.
You ______ be unkind to people who help you.
We ______ thank the workers who keep our town clean.
You ______ forget the service of the military.
Answers: 1. should  2. mustn't  3. should  4. mustn't
Activity 4 — Refugee or Immigrant?
Read each sentence and write refugee or immigrant.
This person left home because there was danger and they needed to be safe.
This person moved to a new country to start a new job and a new life.
Answers: 1. refugee  2. immigrant
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “Can you interview me about a job and check how I speak?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my newspaper article and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me five more sentences to practise should and mustn't?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can name at least twenty jobs and roles in society.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can say what a worker does using the Present Simple.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can give advice and state rules with should and mustn't.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can ask interview questions using who, what, where, why and how.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use prepositions like across, towards, near and by.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can write a role reflection, a newspaper article, and an advice poster.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can explain the difference between a refugee and an immigrant.   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 7

Well done! In Unit 7: Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity, you will move from the jobs people do to the feelings people have and who they are inside. You will learn words for emotions and character, and talk about how we behave and treat one another.
Make sure you know all the Unit 6 words on your Word Wall.
Notice how the people in the story felt — proud, kind, welcoming. Feelings will be important in Unit 7!
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 7? Can you get me ready?”
Remember: every person in your town plays a role, and so do you. Be patient with your learning, and celebrate every new word you master! 🌟

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 6: People in Society
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Community Parade”
The Community Parade

It was the morning of the town's Unity Parade in Northern Nigeria. Fatima stood beside her mother, watching people gather across the dusty street. Everyone was getting ready for the big day. Fatima's school had been chosen to make posters about the important jobs in their community.
Fatima held up a sign. “This one says, ‘Engineer — builds bridges and machines.’”
Her friend Bashir smiled. “Mine says, ‘Janitor — keeps our school clean.’”
Fatima looked at her brother. “Yusuf, where is your sign?”
He laughed. “I made two! One for the military, because they protect us, and one for the refugees who moved here last year.”
“That is kind,” their teacher said. “We must show respect to all people — especially those who help us, and those who need our help.”
The class walked towards the centre of town. They passed the bakery, the market, and the mosque, where families waved and took photos. Everyone stood by the road, ready to watch.
The parade began with the mayor, followed by the local governor. People clapped. “He works so hard,” said an old man.
Next came the military, marching proudly. A small boy asked, “Who are they?”
“They protect our country,” his mother said. “You mustn't forget their service.”
Then a woman held up a photo. “This is my son. He is a lawyer now!”
Fatima whispered to Bashir, “What does a lawyer do?”
“He helps people solve their problems,” Bashir answered.
Suddenly, loud music played. A group of dancers went past. Among them was an artist, painting live on a big canvas.
“Wow!” said Bashir. “That is like magic.”
“It is hard work,” their teacher said. “The artist must plan carefully and work with great care.”
“Look!” Fatima pointed. “Here comes the photographer. She is taking pictures of everything.”
The woman smiled. “Say cheese!” They posed and giggled.
Then a group of immigrants came marching. They carried flags and smiled. A young man held a sign: “From Sudan to Nigeria — I found a home.”
Fatima clapped. “Mama, what is the difference between an immigrant and a refugee?”
Her mother explained, “A refugee leaves home to stay safe. An immigrant moves to build a new life. We should welcome both.”
In the next part of the parade, someone shouted, “Here come the helpers!”
A carpenter walked with his tools. A merchant pushed a cart full of fruit. A laborer waved, his shirt dusty from the morning's work. And a janitor walked proudly in his clean uniform.
Fatima's sign said, “Every helper is a hero.”
A boy behind her asked, “But isn't a hero someone who saves people?”
“Yes,” said Fatima. “But heroes are not only in stories. Some clean our schools, fix our roads, or bring us news.”
Just then, a bicycle bell rang. It was a messenger, holding a stack of newspapers.
“Read today's top article!” he shouted. “Local senator visits school and praises the students!”
Their teacher took a paper and read the first line aloud: “Young citizens learn about jobs and respect.”
Back at school, the class talked about everything they had seen.
“What did you learn?” the teacher asked.
“I learned what a tenant is,” said Bashir. “A person who rents a home.”
“And a consumer,” said Fatima. “That is someone who buys things — like me at the shop!”
The teacher smiled. “Good. And remember: everyone has a role. You should thank the people around you. You mustn't ignore them.”
Later that day, Fatima wrote in her journal. She kept it in a personal place where no one else could read it:

“Today I saw artists, janitors, and even the military. Some wore fine clothes. Others wore work shoes. But all of them help us to live, to grow, and to stay safe. We walk past them every day, but today, we noticed them.”
She paused, then added one more line:
“Next time I see the janitor, I will say thank you. Because heroes don't always wear capes. Sometimes, they carry brooms.”
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
Why was Fatima's school making posters?
What did Yusuf make his two signs for, and why?
According to Fatima's mother, what is the difference between a refugee and an immigrant?
Which helpers marched together near the end of the parade?
What news did the messenger shout about, and what was the article's headline?
What did Fatima promise to do the next time she saw the janitor? What did she mean by “heroes don't always wear capes”?
Think and talk
Fatima wrote, “We walk past them every day, but today, we noticed them.” Who are the helpers you walk past in your own town? Have you ever thanked them?
The teacher said, “Everyone has a role.” What role would you like to play in your community when you grow up? Why?
Why do you think the story says “Every helper is a hero”? Do you agree?
Find the words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 6 words and read out the sentence each one is in:
engineer   ·   janitor   ·   military   ·   refugee   ·   governor
lawyer   ·   artist   ·   photographer   ·   immigrant   ·   carpenter
merchant   ·   laborer   ·   hero   ·   messenger   ·   article   ·   senator
tenant   ·   consumer   ·   personal
Now You Try:  Writing
Write three sentences about a helper in your own community (for example, a shopkeeper, a teacher, or a cleaner). Use the Present Simple: “The ______ helps us by ______.”
Imagine you are watching the parade. Write a short journal entry, like Fatima's, about three people you saw and why they matter.
Finish this sentence in your own way, then explain it to someone: “A hero is someone who ______.”

paragraphs: Vocabulary  —  Unit 6: People in Society
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-study word book

These are the twenty important words for Unit 6. They are all about people — the jobs they do and the parts they play in our society. You will meet them in your Lesson, your Grammar file, and in the story “The Community Parade”. Learn a few words each day: read the meaning, say the example out loud, then do the quick practice. Your AI tutor can check your sentences and help you say each word correctly.
Group 1: Community Helpers and Workers
Practise these words
Point to something in your home made of wood. Say: “A carpenter made things from wood, like this.”
Finish the sentence three times, using a different worker each time: “A ______ helps our community by ______.”
Say what each worker does using the Present Simple: “The engineer designs bridges.”
Group 2: Leaders and the Law
Practise these words
Say which of these people makes rules, and which one protects the country.
Finish the sentence: “A lawyer helps people by ______.”
Use should to give advice: “We should thank the ______ for keeping us safe.”
Group 3: People Who Share Stories and News
Practise these words
Say one sentence about a real or made-up hero you admire.
Imagine you are a photographer. Say: “I take pictures of ______.”
Find a newspaper or magazine (or ask your AI tutor). Say what the article is about.
Group 4: People and Where They Belong
Practise these words
Explain the difference between a refugee and an immigrant in your own words.
Say: “When I buy something at the shop, I am a consumer.”
Use personal in a sentence about something that belongs to you.
My Word Wall
Tick each word when you can read it, say it, and use it in a sentence. Come back at the end of the unit and check again!

tables: A person who cleans and takes care of a building.
The janitor keeps our school clean and tidy.
A person who builds things from wood.
The carpenter made a beautiful wooden table.
A person who does hard work, often with their hands.
The laborer helped build the school walls.
A person who designs or builds machines, roads, or buildings.
The engineer drew plans for a new bridge.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 7: Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Seven grammar mini-lessons

This unit has seven small grammar lessons. Read one lesson, try the practice, and then check your answers in the Answer Key at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it! Do one lesson at a time, not all in one day.
Lesson 1:  Present Perfect (for life experiences)
What it means and why it matters

We use the present perfect to talk about things we have done or felt at some time in our life, when the exact time does not matter. It connects the past to now. Saying “I have felt nervous before” shows the feeling happened, and it still helps us understand ourselves today.
How to form it
Use this pattern: subject + have / has + past participle.
Use have with: I, you, we, they.  →  “I have travelled to many places.”
Use has with: he, she, it.  →  “She has never been shy.”
The past participle is the third form of a verb. Regular verbs add -ed (play → played). Irregular verbs change (go → gone, eat → eaten, be → been).
A common mistake
Mistake: “I have eat lunch.”  ✗
Fix: Use the past participle, not the base verb: “I have eaten lunch.”  ✓
Mistake: “She have seen that film.”  ✗   Fix: “She has seen that film.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of have / has as a bridge between your past and now. He, she, and it always take has.
Practice 1
Complete each sentence using the present perfect.
I __________ (never / try) sushi.
He __________ (see) that film three times.
They __________ (travel) to Kenya.
She __________ (not / be) to a concert.
Lesson 2:  Present Continuous for Future Plans
What it means and why it matters
The present continuous is not only for things happening right now. We also use it to talk about future plans that we have already arranged. It tells people what we have decided to do.
How to form it
Use this pattern: subject + am / is / are + verb-ing + a future time word.
Use am with I, is with he / she / it, and are with you / we / they.
Add a time word for the future, like tomorrow, next week, or on Friday.
Example: “We are visiting a cultural fair tomorrow.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “I am go to the fair tomorrow.”  ✗
Fix: Add -ing to the verb: “I am going to the fair tomorrow.”  ✓
Memory tip
A present-continuous future is like a promise you have already made. Always add a future time word so people know you mean later, not now.
Practice 2
Put the verb in the present continuous for a future plan.
I __________ (visit) my grandparents this weekend.
They __________ (have) a picnic next Sunday.
She __________ (join) the football team next month.
We __________ (watch) a film tonight.
Lesson 3:  Past Simple (for finished actions)
What it means and why it matters
We use the past simple to talk about actions that started and finished in the past. It helps us tell stories and share what happened — like “She felt anxious last night.”
How to form it
Regular verbs add -ed:  play → played,  walk → walked.
Irregular verbs change in their own way:  go → went,  see → saw,  eat → ate,  feel → felt.
Add a past time word: yesterday, last night, last week.
A common mistake
Mistake: “She feels anxious last night.”  ✗
Fix: Change the verb to the past: “She felt anxious last night.”  ✓
Memory tip
Yesterday's actions use -ed for regular verbs. For irregular verbs, learn a few each day.
Practice 3
Write the past simple form of the verb.
I __________ (play) football after school yesterday.
They __________ (go) to the zoo last weekend.
She __________ (feel) tired after the party.
He __________ (eat) rice for dinner yesterday.
Lesson 4:  Imperatives for Advice and Safety
What it means and why it matters
Imperatives are sentences that give advice, instructions, or safety tips. They help us tell people clearly what to do, or what not to do, to stay safe and be kind.
How to form it
Start with the base form of the verb: “Be polite.” “Listen to your teacher.”
For negative advice, start with Don't: “Don't be selfish.”
You do not need to say “you” — it is already understood.
Add Always or Please to sound friendly: “Always say thank you.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “You be polite.”  ✗   Fix: Just say “Be polite.”  ✓
Mistake: “Don't be selfishly.”  ✗   Fix: Keep the base word: “Don't be selfish.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of imperatives as friendly commands — a helpful nudge in the right direction. No “you” needed!
Practice 4
Complete each sentence with the correct imperative.
__________ (be) careful when you cross the road.
__________ (not / touch) the hot stove!
__________ (always / say) thank you.
__________ (not / interrupt) when someone is speaking.
Lesson 5:  Modal Verbs for Advice (must, mustn't, should)
What it means and why it matters
Modal verbs like must, mustn't, and should are helping verbs that give advice or strong suggestions. They let us tell people what is necessary or what is a good idea.
How to form it
Use this pattern: subject + modal verb + base form of the verb.
must = strong advice, very important:  “You must wash your hands before eating.”
mustn't = do not do this:  “You mustn't run in the hallway.”
should = a good idea, softer advice:  “You should be gentle with others.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “You must goes outside.”  ✗   Fix: Use the base verb: “You must go outside.”  ✓
Mistake: Using must for gentle advice. Use should instead: “You should relax.”  ✓
Memory tip
must is a strong “you really need to”, mustn't is a warning “stop!”, and should is a friendly “it's a good idea”.
Practice 5
Complete each sentence with must, mustn't, or should.
You __________ eat your vegetables to stay healthy.
You __________ leave your toys on the floor; pick them up!
You __________ say kind things to your friends.
You __________ talk to strangers you do not know.
Lesson 6:  Question Forms
What it means and why it matters
Questions help us learn about each other and the world. Good learners ask good questions! In this unit we ask people how they feel and what they have done.
How to form it
Yes / No questions start with a verb:  “Do you like football?” “Are you happy?”
Wh- questions start with a question word (who, what, where, when, why, how):  “How do you feel today?”
For experiences, use the present perfect:  “Have you ever felt proud?”
Always end a question with a question mark (?).
A common mistake
Mistake: “You are happy?”  ✗   Fix: Swap the order: “Are you happy?”  ✓
Mistake: “How do you feel today” (no mark)  ✗   Fix: “How do you feel today?”  ✓
Memory tip
Each question word is like a key that unlocks new information. Start with a question word or a verb, and never forget the question mark.
Practice 6
Change each statement into a question.
Lesson 7:  Descriptive Collocations
What it means and why it matters

A collocation is a pair of words that often go together and sound natural. For feelings and behaviour, some adjectives are “best friends” with certain nouns, like anxious thoughts or a proud smile. Using them makes your writing richer.
How to form it
Choose a noun: thoughts.
Choose the adjective that fits it best: anxious.
Put them together: anxious thoughts.
Other good pairs: fierce debate, proud smile, gentle voice.
A common mistake
Mistake: “fierce thoughts” — this sounds strange.  ✗
Fix: Choose an adjective that fits: “anxious thoughts.”  ✓  If a pair sounds odd, try another word.
Memory tip
When you find a pair that sounds natural, remember it as a special team of words.
Practice 7
Choose the word that makes the natural collocation.
I had __________ (anxious / happy) thoughts before the exam.
The class had a __________ (fierce / soft) debate about the best game.
She gave me a __________ (sad / proud) smile after my presentation.
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Present Perfect)
1. have never tried   2. has seen   3. have travelled   4. has not (hasn't) been
Practice 2 (Present Continuous for Future)
1. am visiting   2. are having   3. is joining   4. are watching
Practice 3 (Past Simple)
1. played   2. went   3. felt   4. ate
Practice 4 (Imperatives)
1. Be   2. Don't touch   3. Always say   4. Don't interrupt
Practice 5 (Modal Verbs)
1. must   2. mustn't   3. should   4. mustn't
Practice 6 (Question Forms)
Do you like football?
Is she happy?
Did they go to the park?
Practice 7 (Descriptive Collocations)
1. anxious   2. fierce   3. proud
