paragraphs: Unit 7: Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

In this unit you will learn how to talk about emotions (how we feel), behaviour (how we act), and identity (who we are and where our family comes from). You will read about children who feel nervous, curious, proud, and shy — and who learn to be gentle, polite, and generous to others. You will practise saying how you feel, giving kind advice, and describing what makes your own family special. By the end, you will be ready to understand this unit's story, “The Day of the Play,” in which a girl called Tamara finds her courage on stage.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Name many feelings and describe them, using words like nervous, anxious, proud, and curious.
Describe how people behave, using words like gentle, selfish, generous, and polite.
Talk about experiences in your life using the present perfect (“I have felt nervous before.”).
Talk about plans using the present continuous for the future (“We are visiting a fair tomorrow.”).
Tell about the past using the past simple (“She felt anxious last night.”).
Give advice and safety tips using imperatives and modal verbs (“Always be polite.” / “You should be gentle.”).
Ask and answer questions about feelings (“Have you ever felt proud?”).
Talk about your family's ethnic background and a tradition you are proud of.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and tick your Word Wall.
Work through the seven mini-lessons in the Grammar file, one at a time — never all at once!
Read the Story file, “The Day of the Play,” and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned, and get ready for Unit 8.
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Reading 1 — The Day Before the Test
Read this text slowly. When you meet a feelings word, say it out loud.

Amina sat quietly at the table. Tomorrow was her big spelling test, and she felt nervous. Her thoughts were anxious ones: “What if I forget the words?” Her older brother Yusuf saw her worried face. He was always gentle with her. “Don't be so hard on yourself,” he said kindly. “You have studied every day. I have felt nervous before a test too, and it was fine.” Amina was still doubtful, but she felt a little braver. After Maghrib prayer, Mama brought her warm milk and sat with her. “Be polite to your worry,” Mama said with a smile, “then tell it to go to sleep.” Amina laughed. The next day she felt proud — she remembered every single word.
After reading, answer:
Why did Amina feel nervous?
Find two words in the text that describe feelings, and two words that describe how someone behaves.
How did Yusuf help his sister? Was he selfish or generous with his time?
Have you ever felt nervous before something at school? What did you do?
Reading 2 — Where My Family Comes From
Read this short text about identity:

My name is Sagal, and I am proud of who I am. My family is from a small town near the coast. We speak Somali at home and English at school. On special days, my grandmother tells stories about her childhood, and Mama cooks sweet xalwo for our guests. My friends come from many ethnic groups — some families are from the city, some from farming villages, and some from other countries. We are all different, and I think that is wonderful. Being kind and generous to each other is the most important thing. My grandmother always says, “A guest is a gift.”
After reading, answer:
What languages does Sagal speak, and where?
What does the word ethnic mean in this text?
What does Sagal's grandmother mean by “A guest is a gift”?
What is one tradition your family is proud of? Tell someone about it.
Listening 1 — Getting Ready for the Play
Listen to this short talk. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)

“Good morning, class. Tomorrow is our school play, and I know some of you feel nervous. That is completely normal! Remember three things. First, you have practised hard, so you are ready. Second, be gentle and polite to each other backstage — don't be selfish with the costumes. Third, if you make a small mistake, just smile and keep going. I am so proud of every one of you.”
After listening, answer:
What three things does the teacher tell the class to remember?
Which feelings word and which behaviour word can you hear in the talk?
The teacher says, “Don't be selfish with the costumes.” What kind of sentence is this? (See Grammar Lesson 4.)
Listening 2 — A Chat About Feelings
Listen to two friends talking. Notice the questions they ask.
Hodan: “How do you feel today, Layla?”
Layla: “I feel curious! We are visiting a cultural fair tomorrow. Have you ever been to one?”
Hodan: “Yes, I have. I went last year, and I felt so proud to show my family's food. Why are you curious?”
Layla: “Because I have never tried food from so many different places before!”
After listening, answer:
How does Layla feel, and why?
Find one question that uses “Have you ever…?” (See Grammar Lesson 1.)
Find the sentence that talks about a plan for tomorrow. (See Grammar Lesson 2.)
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — How Do You Feel?
Practise talking about your feelings. Say a sentence for each one, and add a reason with because:
“Today I feel ______ because ______.”
“Yesterday I felt ______ because ______.”
“I have felt ______ before, when ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you use a feelings word? Did you give a reason? Did you speak clearly?
Speaking 2 — Give Kind Advice
Imagine a friend feels nervous or shy. Give them three pieces of kind advice using should, must, and an imperative:
“You should ______.”
“You must ______.”
Speaking Game — Feelings Charades
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
Choose a feelings word (like terrified, curious, or proud) but keep it secret.
Show the feeling with your face and body — no words!
The other person guesses: “Are you feeling ______?” Then swap turns.
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — A Feelings Blog Post

Write a short blog post (four or five sentences) called “A Day I Felt ______.” Choose a feeling such as anxious, curious, or proud. Tell what happened, how you felt, and what you did. Try to use the past simple and one present perfect sentence.
Writing 2 — A Mini-Guide: How to Be Kind at School
Write a little guide titled “How to Be Polite and Kind at School.” Write five pieces of advice using imperatives and modal verbs. For example:
“Always say please and thank you.”
“You should share, and never be selfish.”
“Don't be rough — be gentle with younger children.”
Writing 3 — Proud of My Identity
Write three or four sentences about your family's background or a tradition you are proud of. You could write about a special food, a language you speak, or a celebration like Eid.
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Activity 1 — Match the Feeling
Match each feeling word to what a person might say. Write the pairs.
terrified  —  a) “I really want to know what is inside that box!”
curious  —  b) “There is a huge spider on the wall!”
proud  —  c) “I worked so hard, and I finished it all by myself.”
shy  —  d) “I don't want to speak in front of everyone.”
Answers: 1-b   2-a   3-c   4-d
Activity 2 — Behaviour: Kind or Unkind?
Write kind or unkind for each action.
Sharing your snack with a friend who has none.
Taking all the toys and not letting others play.
Speaking gently to a new student.
Laughing at someone who made a mistake.
Answers: 1. kind (generous)   2. unkind (selfish)   3. kind (gentle/polite)   4. unkind
Activity 3 — Fill in the Feeling
Complete each sentence with a feelings word from this unit.
Before the big test, I felt ______.
When I saw the huge dog, I was ______.
I finished my project all by myself, so I felt ______.
The new girl was too ______ to talk to anyone.
Sample answers: 1. nervous / anxious   2. terrified   3. proud   4. shy
Activity 4 — Word Building: Adjectives to Adverbs
Many feelings and behaviour words can become adverbs. Add -ly (and watch the spelling!).
nervous  →  ______
polite  →  ______
gentle  →  ______
frequent  →  ______
Answers: 1. nervously   2. politely   3. gently (drop the e, add -ly)   4. frequently
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you any time of day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand the present perfect. Can you explain it another way?”
Practise speaking: “Can you listen to me say how I feel and help me?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my writing and tell me one thing to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me five more sentences using modal verbs?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can name and describe many feelings.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can describe how people behave (gentle, selfish, generous, polite).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use the present perfect to talk about my experiences.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can talk about plans with the present continuous.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can give advice with imperatives and modal verbs.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can ask and answer questions about feelings.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can talk about my family's background and a tradition I am proud of.   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 8
Next you will study Unit 8: Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items — all about the objects we use at home and at school and how they work.
Make sure you know all the Unit 7 words on your Word Wall.
Look around your home and name five machines or tools in English (you already know one: the dishwasher!).
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 8? Can you get me ready?”
Well done — understanding feelings and being kind takes practice, just like learning English. Be patient with yourself, and celebrate every new word you learn! 🌟

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 7: Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Day of the Play”
The Day of the Play
Tamara stood behind the school curtain, holding her costume. She looked out at the rows of chairs, all filled with parents. Her hands shook a little.
“I'm so nervous,” she whispered.
“You'll be great,” said her friend Musa. “You've practised every single line.”

Tamara tried to smile. This was the first time she had ever been in a school play. She had the main part — the kind queen who helps people in need. Her teacher had told her, “Tamara, you should be proud. You've worked so hard.”
Still, Tamara felt anxious. What if she forgot her words? What if the lights were too bright?
“You mustn't be so hard on yourself,” said Musa. “Just be gentle and speak politely, like the queen in the story.”
Tamara nodded. “Thanks. I'll try.”
Earlier that week, the whole class had worked hard on the costumes. Musa wore a lion mask. Tamara's little brother, Zuko, came to watch with their parents.
At home the night before, Zuko had told her, “Don't be shy, Tamara. You always help me with my homework. You can talk in front of people.”
Tamara had laughed. “Talking to you is much easier!”
“But you've done harder things,” Zuko said. “Remember the fierce rainstorm? You helped Mama carry the water buckets in the wind. That was risky!”
She smiled now as she remembered that day.
The lights in the hall came on. The show began.
First, the lion walked onto the stage. Musa frequently made the crowd laugh with his funny, humorous roars. The queen had to stop him.
Tamara stepped out. Her voice was quiet at first, but it grew stronger and stronger.
“You must not act wild,” she told the lion, in a serious voice. “We must be polite and kind.”
The audience clapped.

As the play went on, Tamara forgot to feel anxious. She looked at the people in front of her — mothers in colourful ethnic dresses, children from many different backgrounds, and fathers with proud smiles. She felt calm.
At the end, the queen gives her crown to a poor child. Tamara bent down and said softly, “This crown is not just for me. A good leader must be generous.”
The whole crowd stood up and cheered.
Backstage, the teacher clapped. “Tamara, you were amazing!”
Tamara grinned. “I don't feel shy anymore.”
After the play, the students helped tidy up. Tamara and Musa went to wash the paint brushes in the art-room sink. “Let's put these in the dishwasher,” said Musa, pointing at the sink.
“You know that's not a real dishwasher!” Tamara said, laughing.
Outside, a new student stood alone near the wall. She looked terrified. Tamara walked over and said, “Hi, I'm Tamara. Did you like the play?”
The girl nodded but did not speak.
“She seems doubtful,” whispered Musa.
“She's probably just curious, or maybe nervous,” Tamara said. “Let's be kind. It would be selfish to leave her on her own.”
They brought her a chair and a cool drink.
“You were very gentle on the stage,” the girl said at last.
“Thank you!” Tamara smiled. “You're welcome to play with us next time.”
That night, Tamara wrote in her notebook:

“Today I was scared, but I found my courage. I used a gentle voice and I spoke politely. I have felt anxious before, but now I know it's okay. I have also been shy, but helping someone made me feel better. I have learned that being kind is much better than being selfish. I'm not just proud of the play — I'm proud of how I behaved.”
Her mother walked in. “You're still awake?”
“I'm writing about my day,” Tamara said.
“You should be proud,” her mother said gently. “You've grown in so many ways.”
Tamara closed her notebook and turned off the light. She smiled and whispered, “I'm ready for tomorrow.”
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
Why did Tamara feel nervous and anxious at the start of the story?
What kind advice did Musa give Tamara before the play?
What brave, risky thing had Tamara done during the fierce rainstorm?
What did the queen do with her crown at the end, and what does this tell us about being generous?
How did Tamara help the new girl outside? Why did she say it would be selfish to leave her alone?
Think and talk
How did Tamara feel at the start of the play? How did she feel by the end? What changed?
Tamara wrote, “Being kind is much better than being selfish.” Do you agree? Why?
When have you felt nervous but did something brave anyway? Tell someone about it.
How do you make a new person feel welcome, the way Tamara did?
Find the words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 7 words and say the sentence they are in:
nervous · anxious · proud · gentle · polite · fierce
risky · frequent · humorous · serious · ethnic · generous
dishwasher · terrified · doubtful · curious · shy · selfish
Now You Try:  Writing
Write four or five sentences about a time you felt nervous but were brave, just like Tamara. Use at least two feelings words and one present perfect sentence (for example, “I have felt shy before.”).

Imagine you meet a new student who is shy or terrified on their first day. Write what you would say and do to be kind and generous, using imperatives or modal verbs (for example, “You should sit with us.”).

paragraphs: Vocabulary  —  Unit 7: Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-study word book

These are the important words for Unit 7. Learn a few words each day. For every word, read the meaning, say the example out loud, and then do the quick practice. Your AI tutor can check your sentences and help you say the words correctly.
Group 1: Feelings and Emotions
Practise these words
Finish the sentence three times with different words: “Today I feel ______ because ______.”
Which is stronger — nervous or terrified? Say a sentence for each one.
Say one thing that makes you feel curious. Record yourself.
Group 2: How People Behave
Practise these words
Say one kind way to behave and one unkind way, using two words from the table.
Finish the sentence: “A good friend is ______ and ______.”
Act out being polite. What do you say? Record yourself.
Group 3: Identity and Everyday Life
Practise these words
Say one sentence about a tradition from your family's ethnic background.
Name one thing that is risky, and say why we should be careful.
Look around your home. Which machines do you see? (You know one already: the dishwasher!)
Group 4: Word Building — Adverbs
Many of our adjectives become adverbs (words that tell us how something is done) by adding -ly. Watch the spelling carefully!
Adjective  →  Adverb
nervous → nervously · polite → politely · frequent → frequently · gentle → gently · serious → seriously
Practise

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 8: Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Seven grammar mini-lessons
This unit has seven small grammar lessons. Read one lesson, try the practice, and then check your answers at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1:  Defining Relative Clauses (who, that, which)
What it means and why it matters

A defining relative clause is an extra part of a sentence that tells us exactly which person or thing we mean. For example, in “the tool that joins papers,” the words “that joins papers” tell us it is a stapler. This helps us describe the tools and machines in this unit clearly.
How to form it
who → for people:  “She is the teacher who helps us.”
which → for things and animals:  “The car which is parked outside is blue.”
that → for people, animals, or things:  “A machine that heats food is a microwave.”
Tip on the pattern: start with a noun, add who / that / which, then add the extra information — “The crew that worked hard finished early.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “The teacher which helps us is nice.”  ✗
Fix: use who for people: “The teacher who helps us is nice.”  ✓
Also remember to include the relative word. Mistake: “The girl runs fast is my friend.” ✗  Fix: “The girl who runs fast is my friend.” ✓
Memory tip
Think: who for people (like a superhero), which for things (like your favourite toy), and that for almost anything.
Practice 1
Fill in the blank with who, that, or which:
The film ______ was on TV last night was funny.
The girl ______ won the race is my sister.
I have a machine ______ heats food quickly.
The book ______ you lent me was amazing.
The nurse ______ helps sick people is kind.
Lesson 2:  Using “have to” for Obligation
What it means and why it matters
When we say we have to do something, we mean it is necessary — we must do it. This unit uses “have to” for safety rules, like wearing gloves or being careful with sharp tools.
How to form it
Subject + have to / has to + the base form of the verb.
I / you / we / they → have to:  “We have to wear equipment to stay safe.”
he / she / it → has to:  “She has to finish her homework.”
Questions: “Do you have to go?”   Negative: “I don't have to go.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “She have to clean the attic.” ✗    Fix: “She has to clean the attic.” ✓
Also use the base verb: say “have to go”, not “have to going”.
Memory tip
Think of “have to” as your must-do list. If it is on the list, you have to do it!
Practice 2
Complete each sentence with have to or has to and the verb in brackets:
I ______ (finish) my homework before dinner.
She ______ (take) the bus to school every day.
We ______ (wear) helmets when riding bikes.
They ______ (clean) the classroom after school.
______ he ______ (help) his sister with her project?
Lesson 3:  Future Tense with “will” for Promises
What it means and why it matters
We use will to talk about the future, especially to make a promise. Saying “I will” shows that we mean to keep our word, like promising to keep your folder tidy.
How to form it
Subject + will + the base form of the verb.
“I will keep my folder tidy.”
“We will build something useful.”
Negative: add not → “I will not forget.”  Short form: “I won't forget.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “I will keeps my room tidy.” ✗    Fix: use the base verb: “I will keep my room tidy.” ✓
Do not forget the subject: say “I will clean my room”, not “Will clean my room”.
Memory tip
Think of will as a “promise helper”. Whenever you promise something, “will” is right there to help.
Practice 3
Complete each sentence with the correct form using will:
I ______ (help) you with your project.
She ______ (not forget) to bring snacks.
They ______ (clean) the classroom tomorrow.
We ______ (be) friends forever.
Lesson 4:  The Past Simple Tense
What it means and why it matters
The past simple tells about actions that already happened and are finished. It helps us tell stories, like when the class found old tools in the attic.
How to form it
Regular verbs: add -ed → play → played, jump → jumped.
Verbs ending in -e: add just -d → like → liked.
Irregular verbs: you must learn these → go → went, find → found, have → had.
A common mistake
Mistake: “I find my keys yesterday.” ✗    Fix: “I found my keys yesterday.” ✓
Mistake with irregular verbs: “She goed to the store.” ✗    Fix: “She went to the store.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of the -ed ending as a “time stamp” that says: this already happened!
Practice 4
Write the past simple form of the verb in brackets:
I ______ (find) a coin on the ground.
They ______ (play) games all afternoon.
She ______ (go) to the market last week.
We ______ (visit) our friends on Saturday.
He ______ (cook) breakfast this morning.
Lesson 5:  Personal Pronouns and Tense
What it means and why it matters
Personal pronouns — I, you, he, she, it, we, they — take the place of a noun so we do not repeat it. We also change the verb to show past or future. This keeps our stories clear.
How to form it
Choose the right pronoun for the person or thing (she for a girl, it for an object).
Past: use the past verb → “She baked cookies.”
Future: use will + base verb → “We will travel next summer.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “She is my brother.” ✗    Fix: a brother is a boy → “He is my brother.” ✓
Mistake: mixing tenses → “I will played soccer yesterday.” ✗    Fix: “I played soccer yesterday.” ✓
Memory tip
Pronouns are the “stars” of your sentence, and tense is “time travel”: back to the past, or forward to the future.
Practice 5
Fill in the correct pronoun and the right past or future verb:
______ (I) ______ (go) to the zoo last weekend.
______ (We) ______ (play) football tomorrow.
______ (He) ______ (watch) a film last night.
______ (She) ______ (finish) her homework later today.
Lesson 6:  Cause and Effect Connectors (because, so that)
What it means and why it matters
These connectors explain why something happens or for what purpose. They make our ideas clearer and join two thoughts together.
How to form it
because → gives the reason:  “She was late because she missed the bus.”
so that → gives the purpose:  “I study hard so that I can pass my exams.”
You can use so for a more casual purpose: “I wear a jacket so it won't be cold.”
A common mistake
Mistake: leaving out the connector → “He wore a mask it was dusty.” ✗
Fix: “He wore a mask because it was dusty.” ✓   And do not swap them: use because for a reason, so that for a purpose.
Memory tip
because answers “Why?”   ·   so that answers “For what purpose?”
Practice 6
Fill in the blank with because or so that:
I bring my umbrella ______ it might rain.
She went to bed early ______ she could wake up fresh.
He loves to paint ______ it makes him happy.
They wore helmets ______ they would be safe while biking.
Lesson 7:  Imperatives (Giving Instructions)
What it means and why it matters
Imperatives are command words that tell someone what to do. We use them for clear instructions, like “Use your tools safely” or “Put the parts away”.
How to form it
Start with the base verb — no subject needed: “Clean your room.”
Add details: “Carry the books to the library.”
Be polite with “please”: “Please put your shoes away.”
Say “don't” for a negative: “Don't run in the hallway.”
A common mistake
Mistake: adding an “s” → “Uses your utensils properly.” ✗
Fix: imperatives use the base verb → “Use your utensils properly.” ✓  Do not add a subject either: say “Clean your room”, not “You clean your room”.
Memory tip
Imperative = Verb + what you want them to do. It is like giving a friendly command.
Practice 7
Write the correct imperative using the verb in brackets:
______ your hands before eating.  (Wash)
______ your toys after playing.  (Put away)
______ your homework every day.  (Complete)
______ carefully with the sharp hardware.  (Work)
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Relative clauses)
1. that / which   2. who   3. that / which   4. that / which   5. who
Practice 2 (have to / has to)
1. have to finish   2. has to take   3. have to wear   4. have to clean   5. Does … have to help
Practice 3 (will)
1. will help   2. will not forget (won't forget)   3. will clean   4. will be
Practice 4 (past simple)
1. found   2. played   3. went   4. visited   5. cooked
Practice 5 (pronouns and tense)
I went to the zoo last weekend.
We will play football tomorrow.
He watched a film last night.
She will finish her homework later today.
Practice 6 (because / so that)
1. because   2. so that   3. because   4. so that
Practice 7 (imperatives)
1. Wash   2. Put away   3. Complete   4. Work

paragraphs: Unit 8: Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 8! This unit is all about the tools, machines, and everyday items we use at home, at school, and at work. You will learn the names of many useful objects — from a stapler and a microwave to a telescope and a helicopter. You will also learn how to describe what these things do, how to give safe instructions for using them, and how to talk about working together as a crew. By the end of the unit, you will be ready to read and enjoy this unit's mystery story, “The Attic Clue”, about a class in Rwanda who discover old tools in a dusty attic and build something amazing together.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Name and describe many tools, machines, and everyday items in English.
Describe an object using adjectives and a relative clause with who, that, or which.
Say what people have to do to stay safe when using tools and machines.
Make promises about the future using will.
Tell a short story about the past using the past simple tense.
Give clear instructions using imperatives, like “Use”, “Put”, and “Clean”.
Explain reasons using because and so that.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day.
Work through the seven mini-lessons in the Grammar file.
Read the Story file, “The Attic Clue”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Reading 1 — The Right Tool for the Job
Read this short text:

Every job needs the right tool. When Aisha wants to join her homework pages together, she uses a stapler. When her mother wants to heat the soup quickly, she uses the microwave. When Dad carries his work papers to the office, he puts them in a briefcase. Big jobs need big machines. In a factory, heavy machinery helps people build cars and lift boxes. On the farm, a strong crew works together with their tools. The right tool, used the right way, makes hard work easy — and it keeps us safe too.
After reading, answer:
Which tool does Aisha use to join her pages together?
What does her mother use to heat the soup?
Why does the text say the right tool “keeps us safe”? What do you think?
Name one tool or machine in your own home and say what job it does.
Reading 2 — A Look at the Stars
Read this short text:

On a clear night in the desert, the sky is full of stars. Long ago, people could only look up with their eyes. Then someone built a telescope — a tool that helps us see things that are very far away. With a telescope, you can see the craters on the moon and the rings around a planet. A telescope is a machine that brings the sky closer. It shows us that the world is bigger and more wonderful than we thought.
After reading, answer:
What does a telescope help us to do?
Name two things you can see with a telescope, according to the text.
Finish this sentence about the telescope: “A telescope is a machine that ______.”
Listening 1 — How to Use the Microwave Safely
Listen to this short talk. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)

“Hello! Today I will show you how to use a microwave safely. First, put your food in a bowl that is safe for the microwave. Never use metal, because metal can cause sparks. Next, close the door and set the time. You have to stay close so that the food does not get too hot. When it beeps, open the door carefully — the bowl will be hot! Use a cloth to take it out. Stir the food with a utensil before you eat. Enjoy your warm meal!”
After listening, answer:
Why should you never put metal in the microwave?
What do you have to do while the food is heating? (Grammar Lesson 2 will help you.)
Why should you use a cloth to take out the bowl?
List the steps in the correct order. Which words are imperatives (command words)?
Listening 2 — The Helper Vehicles Song
Listen and join in:
“Up goes the helicopter, spinning in the sky. / Fast comes the ambulance, hurry, hurry by! / Big machines to help us, working night and day. / Thank you to the people who keep us safe this way!”
Which machine flies up in the sky?
Which vehicle takes people to hospital quickly?
Who are the people the song says “thank you” to?
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — Describe an Everyday Item
Choose an object from your Vocabulary list. Describe it without saying its name, and let someone guess. Use a relative clause:
“It is a machine that heats food quickly.”  (microwave)
“It is a tool that joins papers together.”  (stapler)
“It is a vehicle that takes sick people to hospital.”  (ambulance)
Now describe three objects of your own. Record yourself and listen back.
Speaking 2 — Safety Rules
Tell a partner or your AI tutor three safety rules, using have to:
“We have to wear gloves when we clean the attic.”
“You have to be careful with sharp hardware.”
“We have to ______.”  (your own idea)
Speaking Game — What Am I?
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person thinks of a tool, machine, or everyday item.
The others ask questions: “Is it a machine that ______?” or “Do you use it in the kitchen?”
Keep asking until someone guesses the object. Then swap turns.
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — An Object Description Poster
Choose a useful tool or item (for example a stapler, a shield, or a microwave). Draw it, then write three sentences about it. Use at least one adjective and one relative clause:
“This is my microwave. It is a white machine that heats food quickly. I use it to warm my soup.”
Writing 2 — Instructions: How to Use It Safely
Write four steps to explain how to use one tool or machine safely. Use imperatives and have to:
Title: “How to Use a ______ Safely”
Include one sentence with “You have to ______ so that ______.”
Writing 3 — A Future Promise
Write two promises about looking after your things, using will:
“I will keep my folder tidy.”
“We will ______.”
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Activity 1 — Match the Tool to the Job
Match each item to what it does. Write the letter next to the number.
stapler
microwave
telescope
ambulance
a) heats food quickly   b) takes sick people to hospital   c) joins papers together   d) helps you see the stars
Answers: 1. c   2. a   3. d   4. b
Activity 2 — Choose the Right Word
Fill each blank with a word from the unit.
Flour is an ______ for making bread.  (ingredient / telescope)
The knight held a ______ to protect himself.  (shield / folder)
We found old toys in the ______ at the top of the house.  (crew / attic)
The factory has big ______ that helps make cars.  (machinery / cocoa)
Answers: 1. ingredient   2. shield   3. attic   4. machinery
Activity 3 — Finish the Relative Clause
Complete each sentence with “who”, “that”, or “which”.
A microwave is a machine ______ heats food quickly.
A crew is a group of people ______ work together.
This is the briefcase ______ my dad takes to work.
Answers: 1. that (or which)   2. who   3. that (or which)
Activity 4 — Critical Thinking
Talk or write about these questions. There is no single right answer — explain your reasons with because and so that.
Which item is the most important in your home? Why?
What would happen if we had no tools or machines?
Why does a crew often work better than one person alone?
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “How do I say this word? Can you listen and help me?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my writing and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more questions about relative clauses?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can name and describe many tools, machines, and everyday items.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can describe an object using “who”, “that”, or “which”.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can say what people “have to” do to stay safe.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can make a promise using “will”.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can tell a short past story using the past simple.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can give clear instructions using imperatives.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
Getting Ready for Unit 9
Well done — you have finished Unit 8! Next you will study Unit 9: Places, People, and Plans, where you will learn to talk about different places, the people who work there, and how to make plans.
Make sure you know all the Unit 8 words on your Word Wall.
Keep using English every day, even for a few minutes.
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 9? Can you get me ready?”
Well done — learning a language takes time and practice. Be patient, and celebrate every new word you learn! 🌟

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 8: Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Attic Clue”
The Attic Clue
High in the green hills of Rwanda, a small village school sat beside tall trees and rolling farms. It had three classrooms, a dusty library, and a small attic just under the roof.
One morning, the teacher came in carrying a big box. “This week,” she said, “we will work together as a crew. We will clean and explore the attic. Then you will write a story about what you find.”
The students gasped. The attic had been locked for years. What secrets could be up there?
“I will open it tomorrow,” said the teacher. “But we have to wear safety equipment — gloves and masks — because the attic may be dusty.”
The next day, the students put on gloves and cloth masks. They took flashlights, brooms, and a folder to hold any papers they found.
Jalia climbed the ladder first. “It is dark,” she said, “but it is not scary.”
Her friend Theo followed close behind. “Look — there is a big curtain covering something!”
They pulled the curtain away and found an old wooden table covered with tools: a stapler, some broken pens, and even a small telescope.
“Look at this!” said Jalia. She pointed to a briefcase with stickers of aeroplanes and stars.
They opened it and found drawings of machines and a note that said: “For the student who will build the future.”
Downstairs, the teacher smiled. “This school was started long ago by a woman who loved science. Perhaps she left this for someone special.”
“We want to build something!” said Theo.
“Then you must plan like a real team,” said the teacher. “Choose your tools and your resources, and work together.”
The students picked their favourite items. “We will use the machinery from the old art room,” said Theo. “It still works.”
“I will clean all the parts,” said Jalia, “and we will write an article about it.”
“You have to be careful with sharp things,” warned the teacher. “Use the hardware safely.”
“We will,” the whole class promised.
That night, Jalia wrote in her notebook: “We found a dusty attic and old tools. We have to clean everything. Tomorrow, we will build something useful.”
The next morning, the crew met early. They carried the parts in and cleaned the work area. Theo warmed some cocoa in the microwave to share with everyone.
“Do not forget to bring a utensil,” said Jalia. “We cannot stir it with our fingers!” She handed out spoons, and everyone laughed.
Little by little, they built a small toy helicopter. It had buttons and even a tiny shield made of plastic. “This shield is for safety!” said Theo.
They tested it outside. It lifted off, spun in the air, and landed gently on the grass.
A young boy clapped his hands. “It flies!”
“Let us write a poster,” said Jalia. “We will describe what it does and how we built it.” Together they wrote:
“This is a flying machine that uses clean energy. It has a shield that protects its wires. It was built by a crew who worked hard together.”
At the school fair, parents and children came to see the project. One mother said, “My son wants to be an inventor now!”
A visiting teacher asked, “Where did you learn to do this?” Theo grinned. “In the attic!”
In the afternoon, an ambulance drove past the school. A student asked, “Who drives that?”
“Someone who helps sick people,” said Jalia, “like a nurse or a doctor.” “We have to learn about safety too,” added Theo, “so that we do not need an ambulance.”
Later, the class packed up. “Put the tools in the folder,” said the teacher, “and then clean up.”
Jalia asked, “Should we wash the spoons in the sink?” “No,” said the teacher, smiling. “Use the new dishwasher the school bought last week!” Everyone laughed — it was their first time using it.
At home that night, Jalia brushed her teeth with her new toothbrush and toothpaste. She looked at her reflection and whispered, “I helped build a flying machine.”
Her mother came in. “Did you learn something today?” she asked.
“Yes,” said Jalia. “I learned that tools help us — but teamwork builds the best things of all.”
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
Where is the school in the story? What was hidden at the top of it?
What safety equipment did the crew have to wear, and why?
What did the children find inside the briefcase?
What did the crew build together? What was its plastic shield for?
What did Jalia say she learned at the end of the story?
Think and talk
The note said, “For the student who will build the future.” What do you think it meant?
Why do you think the class worked better as a crew than they would have worked alone?
The teacher made them wear masks and be careful with sharp tools. Why are safety rules important?
If you found a briefcase of old drawings, what would you want to build? Why?
Find the words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 8 words and say the sentence they are in:
attic
crew
equipment
curtain
stapler
telescope
briefcase
machinery
microwave
helicopter
shield
ambulance
Now You Try:  Writing
Write three sentences about a time you built or fixed something. Use the past simple: “First I ______. Then I ______.”
Imagine your own class finds a mystery box in the attic. Write what is inside and what you will build with it.
Write a short poster about your invention, like the crew did. Use a relative clause: “This is a machine that ______.”

paragraphs: Vocabulary  —  Unit 8: Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-study word book

These are the important words for Unit 8. Learn a few words each day. For every word, read the meaning, say the example out loud, and then do the quick practice. Your AI tutor can check your sentences and help you say the words correctly.
Group 1: Tools and Equipment
Practise these words

Practise these words

Finish the sentence: “In my kitchen, we use a ______ to ______.”

Group 3: Work and Transport
Practise these words

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 9: Places, People, and Plans
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Eight grammar lessons

This unit has eight short grammar lessons. Read one lesson, try the practice, and then check your answers at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it! Do one or two lessons a day, not all at once.
Lesson 1:  Would like to
What it means and why it matters

“Would like to” is a polite way to say what you want, or to invite someone to do something. It is a kinder, softer way of saying “I want to”. We use it a lot when we are being friendly and respectful.
How to form it
Use: would like to + the base verb (the plain form of the action).
“I would like to visit the museum.”
“She would like to play football.”
To invite someone: “Would you like to come with us?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “I would like visit the park.”  ✗
Fix: Don't forget the little word to: “I would like to visit the park.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think: “would like to + do”. The word “to” builds a tiny bridge to the action.
Practice 1
Complete each sentence with “would like to” and the verb in brackets.
She ______ (visit) her grandparents this weekend.
They ______ (play) football after school.
______ you ______ (join) us for lunch?
Lesson 2:  Going to (Future Plans)
What it means and why it matters
We use “going to” to talk about plans — things we have already decided to do in the future. It tells people what we intend to do.
How to form it
Use: subject + am/is/are + going to + base verb.
I → am:  “I am going to visit my aunt.”
He / She / It → is:  “She is going to join the team.”
You / We / They → are:  “We are going to explore the capital.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “I going to play.”  ✗
Fix: Never forget the “to be” word: “I am going to play.”  ✓
Memory tip
“Going to” is like a road sign pointing to the future. First check: “Am I planning to do it?” If yes, use “going to”.
Practice 2
Complete each sentence with the correct form of “going to”.
They ______ (visit) the museum next week.
I ______ (have) a party on Saturday.
She ______ (read) a new book this month.
Lesson 3:  Comparative and Superlative Adverbs
What it means and why it matters
Adverbs tell us HOW something is done (quickly, carefully). We compare them to show that one action is done more, or the most. This helps us describe differences clearly.
How to form it
Short adverbs (one syllable): add -er and -est:  fast → faster → fastest.
Adverbs ending in -ly: use more and most:  carefully → more carefully → most carefully.
Irregular: well → better → best.
A common mistake
Mistake: “He arrived more fast than me.”  ✗
Fix: “fast” is short, so add -er: “He arrived faster than me.”  ✓
Memory tip
Comparative = compare two things (-er / more). Superlative = the super best of all (-est / most).
Practice 3
Write the correct form of the adverb in brackets.
She sings ______ (loud) than her sister.
Out of all the students, he works the ______ (hard).
This book reads ______ (easy) than the last one.
Lesson 4:  Present Continuous for Plans
What it means and why it matters
The present continuous (am/is/are + verb-ing) usually means “happening right now”. But we can also use it for plans that are already arranged for the future, especially when we say WHEN.
How to form it
Use: subject + am/is/are + verb-ing, and add a future time word.
“We are visiting the factories next week.”
“She is studying for her test this weekend.”
“I am meeting my friends tomorrow.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “We are plan a visit.”  ✗
Fix: Add -ing to the verb: “We are planning a visit.”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of it as a “now-and-later” tense. If you can picture it happening, and you add a time word, it becomes a plan.
Practice 4
Complete each sentence with the present continuous form.
I ______ (visit) my grandmother this weekend.
They ______ (watch) a new show tonight.
We ______ (plan) a picnic for Saturday.
Lesson 5:  Imperatives (Instructions)
What it means and why it matters
Imperatives are verbs we use to tell someone what to do. We use them to give directions, instructions, and friendly commands — very useful when you show someone around a place!
How to form it
Start with the base verb. Do not add “you”:  “Take the elevator.” (not “You take the elevator.”)
Add please to be polite:  “Please check the map.”
For a NO, start with don't:  “Don't run in the hallway.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “Not touch that.”  ✗
Fix: Start a negative command with “don't”: “Don't touch that.”  ✓
Memory tip
Imagine you are a friendly captain giving an order. You don't say “You go”, you just say “Go!”
Practice 5
Fill in the blank with the correct imperative (the verb is in brackets).
______ the book on the table. (put)
______ your homework before dinner. (finish)
______ the lights when you leave. (turn off)
Lesson 6:  Question Tags
What it means and why it matters
A question tag is a short question added to the end of a sentence to check if someone agrees. It keeps a conversation friendly: “You're coming, aren't you?”
How to form it
If the sentence is positive, the tag is negative:  “You're happy, aren't you?”
If the sentence is negative, the tag is positive:  “They don't like it, do they?”
Match the pronoun:  “She can swim, can't she?”
A common mistake
Mistake: “You're happy, are you?”  ✗
Fix: Positive sentence needs a negative tag: “You're happy, aren't you?”  ✓
Memory tip
Think of a seesaw: when the sentence goes UP (positive), the tag goes DOWN (negative), and the other way round.
Practice 6
Choose the correct question tag.
You're coming to the party, ______? (aren't you / are you)
They don't like chocolate, ______? (do they / don't they)
She's your friend, ______? (is she / isn't she)
Lesson 7:  Expressions of Emotion
What it means and why it matters
Expressions of emotion let us share how we feel. This makes conversations warmer and helps friends understand each other.
How to form it
Use I'm + feeling:  “I'm excited!”  “I'm nervous.”
Or I feel + feeling:  “I feel hungry.”
Agree with someone by saying:  “Me too!”
A common mistake
Mistake: “I happy.”  ✗
Fix: Always use “I'm” before the feeling: “I'm happy.”  ✓
Memory tip
Feelings need the little word “am”. Say it as “I'm” — “I'm” + how you feel.
Practice 7
Fill in the blank with a suitable emotion word.
I'm ______ to go to the coast! (a happy feeling)
I'm ______ because I lost my book. (a sad feeling)
My friend said she is excited. I said, “______!” (agree)
Lesson 8:  Pronouns and Sentence Starters
What it means and why it matters
Pronouns (I, you, he, she, it, we, they) take the place of names so we don't repeat them. Sentence starters like “We need…”, “Let's…”, and “I feel…” help us begin our ideas clearly.
How to form it
Replace a name with a pronoun:  “Maria likes it.” → “She likes it.”
Begin a plan with a starter:  “Let's go to the museum.”  “We need a map.”
Use I as the doer, me as the receiver:  “I like it.”  “She gave me a book.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “Me want to go.”  ✗
Fix: “Me” cannot be the doer. Use “I”: “I want to go.”  ✓
Memory tip
Pronouns are shortcuts that save time. Sentence starters are keys that unlock the start of your idea.
Practice 8
Complete each sentence using the starter or pronoun in brackets.
______ go to the museum this weekend! (Let's)
______ need to help each other. (We)
______ tired after running. (I feel)
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Would like to)
1. would like to visit   2. would like to play   3. Would … like to join
Practice 2 (Going to)
1. are going to visit   2. am going to have   3. is going to read
Practice 3 (Comparative / superlative adverbs)
1. louder   2. hardest   3. more easily
Note: careful writers say “more easily” (the adverb), though many people say “easier” in speech.
Practice 4 (Present continuous for plans)
1. am visiting   2. are watching   3. are planning
Practice 5 (Imperatives)
1. Put   2. Finish   3. Turn off
Practice 6 (Question tags)
1. aren't you?   2. do they?   3. isn't she?
Practice 7 (Expressions of emotion)
1. excited   2. sad   3. Me too
(Other sensible feeling words are fine too — ask your AI tutor to check yours.)
Practice 8 (Pronouns and sentence starters)
1. Let's   2. We   3. I feel

paragraphs: Unit 9: Places, People, and Plans
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson  ·  The last unit of Year 4
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 9 — the final unit of Year 4! This unit is all about places, people, and plans. You will learn the words we use to talk about travelling: the airport, the station, the museum, the capital city, and much more. You will meet the people we find in busy places, like the customer at a shop and the workers in an office. Most of all, you will learn how to make plans — how to invite a friend, how to say what you are going to do, and how to give clear directions. By the end, you will be able to plan a whole trip in English, just like Amani and Timo do in this unit's story when they explore the city of Mombasa.
What You Will Be Able to Do
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Name many kinds of places — the airport, station, museum, mall, and more.
Talk about the people and movement in busy places, using words like customer and arrive.
Invite someone politely using would like to.
Talk about your future plans using going to and the present continuous.
Compare how people do things using comparative and superlative adverbs (faster, most carefully).
Give clear instructions and directions using imperatives (Take the elevator. Turn left.).
Check what someone thinks using question tags (You're coming, aren't you?).
Plan and describe a trip around your capital city in writing and speaking.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a small group of place words each day.
Work through the Grammar file. There are eight short lessons — do one or two at a time.
Read the Story file, “The Day We Got Lost in Mombasa”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below in order: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to check what you have learned this year.
Part 1:  Reading & Listening
Reading 1 — A Trip to the Capital
Read this text carefully:

Last month, my family took a trip to the capital city. First, we went to the big station and waited for the train. When the train arrived, we found seats near the window and watched the horizon rush past. In the city there was so much to see! We visited a museum full of old maps and tools. Then we walked through a busy neighbourhood, past shops, offices, and a tall building with a glass elevator. Tourism brings many people to the capital, so the streets were full. At the end of the day, we ate at a small restaurant near the entrance of the market. I was tired, but I would like to go again next year.
After reading, answer:
Where did the family go first, before the train arrived?
Name three places they visited in the capital city.
Why were the streets so full? Find the sentence that tells you.
What does the writer want to do next year? Which words tell you?
Reading 2 — Living Near the Equator
Read this short text:

Kenya sits close to the equator, the imaginary line around the middle of the Earth. Near the equator, the sun is very strong and the days are warm all year. People still build, travel, and work hard here. Trains run on the railroad, ships arrive at the coast, and factories make goods for the whole nation. Whether you live in a small neighbourhood or a big capital, there is always a place to go and a plan to make.
After reading, answer:
What is the equator? Explain it in your own words.
Why is it warm all year near the equator?
What do the factories do for the nation?
Listening 1 — Making a Plan (listening script)
Ask your AI tutor to read this out loud while you listen with your eyes closed. Then read it yourself.
Amina: “We're going to explore the capital on Saturday, aren't we?”
Yusuf: “Yes! First we are going to the museum. Then we would like to try the new restaurant near the station. Would you like to come?”
Amina: “I'd love to! Let's arrive early so we have more time. I'm so excited!”
Yusuf: “Me too! I'll bring the map.”
After listening, answer:
What are Amina and Yusuf going to do first?
Find one question tag in the script. What is it checking?
Which words do they use to show they are excited?
Listening 2 — Directions at the Mall (listening script)
Listen as your AI tutor reads the directions. Try to follow them in your head.

“Welcome to the mall! To find the bookshop, take the elevator to the second floor. Turn left and walk down the long hallway. The shop is near the entrance, next to the juice stand. Please walk carefully — the floor can be slippery!”
How do you get to the second floor?
After the elevator, which way do you turn?
Which word tells you HOW to walk? (See Grammar Lesson 5 on imperatives and Lesson 3 on adverbs.)
Part 2:  Speaking
Speaking 1 — Invite a Friend
Use would like to to invite someone. Get ready to say:
“Would you like to visit the museum with me?”
“Would you like to come to the event in my neighbourhood?”
“I would like to try the new restaurant. Would you like to come?”
Record yourself. Check: Did you say “would like to”? Did you sound polite and friendly?
Speaking 2 — Tell Someone Your Plan
Use going to to describe a plan for a trip. Say three sentences:
“On Saturday, I am going to ______.”
“We are going to arrive at the ______ at ______ o'clock.”
“After that, we are going to ______.”
Speaking Game — Where Am I?
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person thinks of a place from the vocabulary list (station, mall, airport, museum …).
The other person asks yes/no questions: “Can you buy food there?” “Do trains stop there?”
Keep asking until you can say: “You are at the ______!” Then swap turns.
Part 3:  Writing
Writing 1 — A Tour Plan for Your Capital City

Imagine you are showing a visitor around your capital city. Write a short paragraph (4–5 sentences) about the tour. Include at least three places, such as a museum, a restaurant, or the airport. Use going to and arrive.
Example opening: “First, we are going to visit the museum. We will arrive at ten o'clock …”
Writing 2 — An Invitation to an Event

Write an invitation to an event in your neighbourhood — maybe an Eid celebration, a market day, or a football match. Use would like to at least twice. Remember to say WHAT the event is, WHERE it is, and WHEN it is.
Writing 3 — A Short Dialogue
Write a short dialogue (6–8 lines) between a customer and a worker at a bike rental shop. Use polite language and at least one imperative (an instruction).
Part 4:  Activities & Exercises
Activity 1 — Match the Place to What You Do There
Match each place (1–6) to the correct action (a–f).
airport
museum
restaurant
station
mall
office
a. eat a meal cooked for you
b. catch a train or a bus
c. work at a desk
d. see old things and learn about history
e. get on an aeroplane
f. shop in many stores under one roof
Answers: 1–e   2–d   3–a   4–b   5–f   6–c
Activity 2 — Choose the Right Grammar
Write the correct choice for each sentence.
We are ______ (going to / would like) explore the capital tomorrow.
Would you ______ (like to / going to) visit the museum?
The train arrived ______ (fast / faster) than the bus.
You're coming to the event, ______ (aren't you / are you)?
______ (Take / Taking) the elevator to the fifth floor.
Answers: 1. going to   2. like to   3. faster   4. aren't you   5. Take
Activity 3 — Fill in the Blanks (vocabulary)
Complete each sentence with a word from the unit.
The main city of a country is its ______.
A person who buys something is a ______.
The line where the sky meets the sea is the ______.
A place where aeroplanes take off and land is an ______.
The area around your home is your ______.
Answers: 1. capital   2. customer   3. horizon   4. airport   5. neighbourhood
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don't understand question tags. Can you explain them a different way?”
Practise speaking: “Can you listen to my plan and check my ‘going to’ sentences?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my tour plan and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me five more sentences with comparative adverbs?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment:  Can I Do It?
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can name many kinds of places in a town or city.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can invite someone politely using “would like to”.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can talk about future plans using “going to”.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can compare actions using adverbs (faster, most carefully).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can give clear instructions using imperatives.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use question tags to check what someone thinks.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can plan and describe a trip in writing.   Yes □   Not yet □
Congratulations — You Finished Year 4!

You have reached the end of the very last unit of Year 4. That is a wonderful achievement! Think about how much you have grown this year. You started Year 4 as a reader and a learner, and now you can plan trips, invite friends, give directions, compare actions, and write whole paragraphs in English. Be proud of yourself — masha'Allah, you have worked hard.
Look back through your Word Walls from this year. Count how many words you can now use. It is a lot!
Read your favourite story again, just for fun. Notice how much easier it feels now.
Thank the people who helped you — your teacher, your family, and your AI tutor.
Well done, and congratulations again. A language grows a little every day — keep going, and see you in Year 5! 🌟

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 9: Places, People, and Plans
Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Day We Got Lost in Mombasa”
The Day We Got Lost in Mombasa
“We're going to the capital, aren't we?” asked Amani, holding her small travel bag.
“Not today,” said her uncle with a smile. “We're visiting Mombasa! It's a special city by the ocean. There's a museum, a station, and even a big restaurant near the sea.”
Amani and her cousin Timo were excited. It was their first time travelling without their parents. Uncle Jabir was in charge.
They arrived at the station earlier than planned. “Let's buy some juice while we wait,” said Timo.
At the juice stand, the customer in front of them bought three bottles. “Take your time,” said the seller kindly.
Timo whispered, “This place is so busy. The neighbourhood feels different from ours.”
Amani nodded. “It's bigger. More tourism, maybe. Look at all the visitors!”
When their train came, they found seats near the window. “I want to see the horizon,” said Amani. “I love how the sea meets the sky.”
In Mombasa, the air was hotter, and the streets were full of colour. “First stop,” said Uncle Jabir, “the museum.”
Inside, they saw old tools, maps, and animal bones. A guide pointed to the wall. “This was found near the equator,” he explained. “The heat there is strong, but people still build and work hard.”
Amani and Timo took photos. “Now, let's go to the mall before lunch,” said Uncle Jabir.
At the mall, they looked for gifts and rode the elevator for fun. “We went up to the fourth floor faster than I thought!” said Timo.
After that, they stopped at a restaurant. “Would you like to try local fish, or chicken and rice?” asked Uncle Jabir.
“I'd like the chicken,” said Timo. “Me too!” added Amani.
“You two always agree,” he said, laughing.
“I feel hungry already,” said Amani.
The waiter walked them to their table. “The entrance is beautiful,” said Amani, looking at the painted doors.
After lunch, Uncle Jabir had a quick call at the office where he once worked. “Wait by the shop near the hallway, okay?”
“Okay,” said Amani. “We'll stay carefully nearby.”
But the shop was full of wonderful souvenirs — little aeroplanes for the airport, capital city keychains, and even a tiny model railroad!
Timo's eyes grew wide. “Let's just look at that shop across the street.”
“But Uncle said to wait,” Amani warned.
“It won't take long,” said Timo.
They crossed the road and looked around. But when they turned back, the building looked different. The entrance had a blue sign now, not a green one.
“This isn't the place!” said Amani, her heart beating fast. “Where's the hallway? Where's the office?”
They were lost.
Amani took a slow breath. “Let's not panic. Let's ask someone at the rental stand,” she said.
A kind woman there smiled. “I saw your uncle go that way. Take the elevator up. His office is on the second floor.”
They thanked her and followed her directions. Carefully, they stepped into the elevator and pressed the button.
When the door opened, they saw Uncle Jabir at the end of the hallway.
“You're going to give me grey hair!” he said, but then he smiled. “You're safe — that's what matters most.”
“We were just curious,” said Timo, looking down.
“Curiosity is good,” Uncle replied gently. “But you must stay careful in busy places, and keep to the plan.”
Back at the train station, they waited with cold drinks.
“We saw so many places today,” said Amani. “The factories, the market, even the coast.”
“I liked the museum the best,” said Timo. “It teaches more than the mall does.”
Uncle nodded. “That's the difference between shopping and learning.”
“Would you like to travel again?” he asked.
“Yes!” they shouted together.
“Maybe next time we'll see the airport,” said Amani.
“Or climb a hill near the equator,” added Timo.
They laughed, feeling tired but happy. As the train pulled away, Amani looked out at the setting sun. “I can still see the ocean,” she said softly, “far away on the horizon.”
After Reading:  Understanding the Story
Answer these questions
Which city did Uncle Jabir take the children to, and why is it special?
What was the first place they visited in Mombasa? What did they see there?
Why did Amani and Timo get lost?
Who helped them find their way back, and what directions did she give?
At the end, which place did Timo like best? What reason did he give?
Think and talk
Amani said, “Let's not panic.” Why was that a wise thing to do when they were lost? What would you do?
Uncle Jabir said, “Curiosity is good, but you must stay careful.” What did he mean? Do you agree?
Timo said the museum “teaches more than the mall does.” Do you agree? Why or why not?
Have you ever visited a big, busy place? How did it feel compared to your own neighbourhood?
Find the words

Write four sentences about a place you would like to visit. Use would like to and going to at least once each.

Year 4 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-study word book

These are the important words for Unit 9. Learn a small group each day. For every word, read the meaning, say the example out loud, and then do the quick practice. Your AI tutor can check your sentences and help you say the words correctly. Tick each word on your Word Wall at the end when you can use it.
Group 1: Places You Travel To
Practise these words
Say the name of your capital city out loud, then use it in a sentence.
Finish the sentence: “At the airport, I saw ______.”
Draw a simple map with a station and a railroad. Label them in English.
Group 2: Places in a Town or City
Practise these words
Name one place in your own neighbourhood and say what people do there.
Finish the sentence: “I would like to visit the ______ because ______.”
Compare two places: “A museum is for ______, but a mall is for ______.”
Group 3: Parts of a Building
Practise these words
Give a direction using two of these words: “Go through the entrance and down the hallway.”
Finish the sentence: “Take the elevator to the ______ floor.”
Say what you could get as a rental on a trip (a bike, a car, an umbrella).
Group 4: People, Movement, and Events
Practise these words
Use ‘arrive’ in a plan: “We are going to arrive at the ______ at ______.”
Describe an event you enjoyed: “At the event, I ______.”
Finish the sentence: “At sunset, the horizon turned ______.”

tables: Travelling to new places for fun or to learn about them.
Tourism brings many visitors to the coast every summer.
A place where aeroplanes take off and land.
We waited at the airport for our flight to Nairobi.
A place where trains or buses stop to pick up people.
We waited at the station for the morning train.
The track that trains travel along.
The railroad runs all the way across the country.
The main city of a country, where the government is.
Nairobi is the capital of Kenya.
A country and all the people who live in it.
Kenya is a proud nation with beautiful places.

A building where you see old or special things and learn about them.
We saw dinosaur bones at the museum.
A big shopping centre with many stores under one roof.
We bought new shoes at the mall.
A place where people buy and eat meals that cooks make.
We had rice and chicken at a new restaurant.
A place where people work at desks.
My mother works in an office in the city.
Buildings where things are made, like clothes or toys.
The factories make shoes for the whole nation.
Our neighbourhood has many trees and kind people.

The door or gate where you go into a place.
The entrance to the museum had big painted doors.
A long path inside a building that joins rooms.
We walked down the hallway to find the office.
A machine that carries people up and down between floors.
We took the elevator to the fifth floor.
Something you pay to use for a short time.
We paid for a bike rental for the whole day.

A person who buys something from a shop or business.
The customer paid for her food and said thank you.
To get to a place after travelling.
We will arrive at the station at four o'clock.
Something special that happens, like a party or a game.
The school event had music, food, and games.
The line far away where the land or sea meets the sky.
We watched the sun go down at the horizon.
The imaginary line around the middle of the Earth.
It is very hot and sunny near the equator.
